Thank you @lalitc375! That's super helpful!

Is there anything else that we should do in this case? Should we improve the error message? Should we change something in the SSA or non-SSA code? Or is this one more inconsistency between SSA and other ways of interacting with the API that we're accepting?

For the context we're implementing a workaround for https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/113482 in Kueue and we're discovering more and more issues and inconsistencies.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
Adding `nullable: true` to the schema does help, however I feel like something unexpected is happening with the validation.

> This issue is not related to SSA, you would have seen this in the non SSA flows.

With regards to the repro steps, the validation error doesn't show up if I remove the map through non SSA update. Neither does it show up if I don't patch the map entry. Neither does it show up if I patch the map entry through SSA.

I'd expect to get the validation error in either all or none of these scenarios.

It's also a bit surprising that this validation doesn't fire if I use a regular array instead of a map and follow the repro steps.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
>With regards to the repro steps, the validation error doesn't show up if I remove the map through non SSA update. Neither does it show up if I don't patch the map entry. Neither does it show up if I patch the map entry through SSA.

I spoke too early. What I wanted to say is that you don't need to perform any kind of update operation in between. Array removal post creation is also failing. There is a validation behaviour difference between server side apply and patch, I will find the reason behind that difference and update it here.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
You're right, with the CRD I provided in the original bug description you don't need a patch to get the validation error. However if you add another field to the CRD, e.g.
```
spec:
  baz: "2" # <--- add this
  arrayMap:
  - key: "0"
    foo: "1"
```

Then the attempt to remove the arrayMap through SSA will only fail if you do the non-SSA patch (or also remove the baz field)
```
spec:
  baz: "2"
# no validation error for removing arrayMap **unless** you patch the arrayMap beforehand
```

Also, you're right that if you don't patch, you *do* get the validation error for regular arrays. However, with the patch in between, I don't get the error.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
Hi @brejman,

Thank you for your patience and for the detailed discussion on this issue. After further analysis and reviewing the logs, I have a much clearer and more precise explanation.

This is not a bug in the Server-Side Apply (SSA) merge logic itself, but rather that the SSA logic, which behaving as designed, can produce an object that is invalid according to the CRD schema, whereas a non-SSA apply produces a different, valid object.

The core of the issue is the difference in the final object sent to the validator.

### Server-Side Apply (SSA) Outcome

When an SSA manager stops managing a field (`arrayMap`) while other fields within the same object (`spec`) are still present, the SSA merge logic sets the field to `null`.

<details>
<summary>Logs for SSA showing `arrayMap: null`</summary>

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
ng object at path "spec", object: map[arrayMap:<nil> baz:test1]
I1106 21:24:49.773292       1 schema.go:182] CRDValidationDebug: Validating object at path "spec", json: {"arrayMap":null,"baz":"test1"}
I1106 21:24:49.773304       1 schema.go:127] CRDValidationDebug: Validating object at path "spec.arrayMap", object: <nil>
I1106 21:24:49.773313       1 type.go:137] CRDValidationDebug: Validating null object at path "spec.arrayMap", nullable in schema: false
```
</details>

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
This results in an object like `{"spec":{"arrayMap":null,"baz":"test1"}}`. The CRD validator correctly rejects this object because the `arrayMap` field is present with a `null` value, but the schema does not have `nullable: true`.

### Non-Server-Side Apply Outcome

When using a traditional client-side `kubectl apply`, the client computes a patch to remove the `arrayMap` field entirely.

<details>
<summary>Logs for non-SSA showing `arrayMap` is absent</summary>

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
```
I1106 21:27:15.961635       1 schema.go:182] CRDValidationDebug: Validating object at path "", json: {"apiVersion":"example.com/v1alpha1","kind":"MyCrd","metadata":{"annotations":{"kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration":"{\"apiVersion\":\"example.com/v1alpha1\",\"kind\":\"MyCrd\",\"metadata\":{\"annotations\":{},\"name\":\"repro\"},\"spec\":{\"baz\":\"test1\"}}\\n"},"creationTimestamp":"2025-11-06T21:21:26Z","generation":5,"managedFields":[{"apiVersion":"example.com/v1alpha1","fieldsType":"FieldsV1","fieldsV1":{"f:metadata":{"f:annotations":{"f:kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration":{}}}},"manager":"kubectl-client-side-apply","operation":"Update","time":"2025-11-06T21:27:15Z"}],"name":"repro","resourceVersion":"2706","uid":"120958ab-2746-40e8-8093-623ac0dd6dc6"},"spec":{"baz":"test1"}}
I1106 21:27:15.961658       1 schema.go:127] CRDValidationDebug: Validating object at path "spec", object: map[baz:test1]
I1106 21:27:15.961669       1 schema.go:182] CRDValidationDebug: Validating obj

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
This results in an object like `{"spec":{"baz":"test1"}}`. The `arrayMap` field is simply absent. This object is valid according to the schema, so the operation succeeds.

### Scenario 1: Create with SSA, then Remove with SSA (No Patch)

When an SSA manager removes the *only* field within an object, the SSA logic prunes the parent object itself to `null`.

<details>
<summary>Logs for SSA showing `spec: null`</summary>

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
This results in an object like `{"spec": null}`. The CRD validator correctly rejects this because the schema defines `spec` as `type: object`, but receives `null`.

### Conclusion

The inconsistent behavior you observed is caused by these two different approaches generating two different final objects. The SSA-generated object is invalid against the schema, while the non-SSA-generated object is valid.

This highlights a challenging interaction between the strict ownership model of SSA and CRD validation. While SSA is functioning correctly, its output in this specific multi-manager scenario (especially when interacting with a non-SSA manager) can lead to validation failures that users might not expect.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text: The immediate workaround is to add `nullable: true` to the schema to accommodate SSA's behavior. For a longer-term solution, we might consider if there's a way to improve this interaction, perhaps by providing more explicit error messages that guide the user on how to resolve the ownership conflict that led to the `null` value.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
May I ask _why_ does SSA behave differently from non-SSA in this case?
I mean, _why_ does it use `null`, instead of just leaving the field unset?

(Apologies if it's a too basic question - I did Google search for "Kubernetes SSA null" but found nothing doc-like - if there is a good reading link, I'll be grateful for that)

Am I guessing right that it's intended to clearly reflect which field manager has cleared the field value?
Is there a convention that a field manager can be stored for a `null`-valued field but not for a "completely absent" field?
Otherwise, what's the rationale behind explicit `null`?

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
Thank you for the explanation. I've gone over all scenarios in this bug again and something still doesn't seem right.

For example, the following will succeed, even though there's clearly a single owner
```
kubectl delete mc repro

kubectl apply --server-side --field-manager A --force-conflicts -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: "example.com/v1alpha1"
kind: MyCrd
metadata:
  name: repro
spec:
  baz: "2"
  arrayMap:
  - key: "0"
    foo: "1"
EOF

kubectl apply --server-side --field-manager A --force-conflicts -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: "example.com/v1alpha1"
kind: MyCrd
metadata:
  name: repro
spec:
  baz: "2"
EOF

kubectl get mc repro -o json --show-managed-fields
```

According to your explanation, I'd expect the update to fail validation.

I'm also not sure where the behavior you mentioned about `null` is documented. I think it may be the following two fragments:

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
> Apply checks if there are any other field managers that also own the field. If the field is not owned by any other field managers, that field is set to its default value (if there is one), or otherwise is deleted from the object. The same rule applies to fields that are lists, associative lists, or maps.

> If you remove a field from a manifest and apply that manifest, Server-Side Apply checks if there are any other field managers that also own the field. If the field is not owned by any other field managers, it is either deleted from the live object or reset to its default value, if it has one. The same rule applies to associative list or map items.

Even if we assume that the default value for a map is null, the field ends up getting deleted. So it'd be doing both, which is against my understanding of the phrase "either or" or "otherwise".

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
I have investigated why an `arrayMap` (associative list) collapses to `null` during Server-Side Apply when one manager omits the list while another manager owns a field of an item inside it.

#### Root Cause: Missing Implicit Ownership
The issue occurs because the `structured-merge-diff` library fails to implicitly attribute ownership of an item's **Key Fields** to a manager that only claims a leaf field inside that item.

**The Bug Scenario:**
*   **Manager A** omits the list (applies `{}`).
*   **Manager B** (e.g., `kubectl patch`) owns a field `spec.arrayMap[key="0"].foo`.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
**Failure Chain:**
1.  The library sees that Manager A released the list item.
2.  It checks if Manager B owns the item container `[key="0"].foo`.
3.  Because Manager B's ownership is recorded strictly as "owning `foo`" (and the library fails to infer that owning `foo` requires owning the container `[key="0"]` and the key `key="0"`), the library determines the item is abandoned.
4.  The item is pruned.
5.  Since the list becomes empty during this process, the reconstruction logic returns a `nil` result for the list field, which is assigned to the parent map as `arrayMap: null`.
6.  **Validation Error:** This `null` value violates the OpenAPI schema (which expects an array), causing the API server to reject the request with `Invalid value: "null"`.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
**Managed Fields (Illustrative State during Failure):**
You can see in the `managedFields` that Manager B *should* own the field, but the library interprets this strictly as a leaf-field ownership, missing the structural dependency on the item key. Manager A's managed fields would also show `f:key: {}` and `.: {}` for the item, but Manager A's apply drops these.

```yaml
# Manager B's managed fields (from patch)
- manager: kubectl-patch
  operation: Update
  fieldsV1:
    f:spec:
      f:arrayMap:
        k:{"key":"0"}:
          f:foo: {}  <-- B owns 'foo'.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
# Manager A's managed fields (from initial apply, before its current apply)
# These fields are what Manager A is attempting to relinquish in the buggy scenario.
- apiVersion: example.com/v1alpha1
  fieldsType: FieldsV1
  fieldsV1:
    f:spec:
      f:arrayMap:
        k:{"key":"0"}:
          .: {}   # A owns the list item itself.
          f:key: {} # A owns the key.
          # f:foo: {} # If A originally owned foo.
      f:baz: {}
  manager: A
  operation: Apply
```

#### Behavior Comparison (Omitted vs. Explicit Null vs. Shared Ownership)

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
*   **Omitted (No Shared Ownership):** If Manager A omits the list and **no other manager** owns any items, the field is pruned completely. The library returns `nil` for the field value, and the parent map reconstruction simply **skips** adding the key. The result is the field is absent (omitted).
*   **Explicit Null:** If Manager A explicitly applies `{"arrayMap": null}`, they claim ownership with a `null` value. The result is explicitly `null`.
*   **Shared Ownership (Not Omitted):** If Manager A and Manager B share ownership of an item in the list (e.g., both claim distinct fields within the same item), and Manager A tries to drop the item (by omitting it from their `ApplyConfiguration`), the item is **not** omitted. Instead, Manager B retains the item and becomes the sole owner of the fields it claims, preserving the structure.

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text: @jpbetz @liggitt  What do you think about the behaviour mentioned in the last comment? Should an item be dropped if a field in that item is owned by another manager?  If we don't drop, who will own the key for that item after the operation?

title: Non-SSA update of a map’s entry prevents deleting the map via SSA
text:
> 5. Since the list becomes empty during this process, the reconstruction logic returns a nil result for the list field, which is assigned to the parent map as arrayMap: null.

Is this the spot to reconsider? Do we have schema information at that point to know the `arrayMap` field is non-nullable? If so, should we be pruning the value from the result rather than assigning an explicit nil? Would that propagate out correctly to the desired end result?

title: DRA: integration-test: cannot set PreAlpha feature gate at emulated version 1.33 but no allocator available for feature set (stable allocator)
text: DRA: integration-test: cannot set PreAlpha feature gate at emulated version 1.33 but no allocator available for feature set (stable allocator)

title: DRA: integration-test: cannot set PreAlpha feature gate at emulated version 1.33 but no allocator available for feature set (stable allocator)
text:
### What happened?

In `scheduler_perf/dra` of integration-test,
we enable each allocator for testing and now override the beta feature = false when allocator is stable and in the test, there is a case that version 1.33 needs to be emulated.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/ec2c7739bc3ccc602f7af67b096d44a9f446122a/test/integration/scheduler_perf/dra/dra_test.go#L60-L65

However, for some beta features including DRAConsumableCapacity, it is PreAlpha in 1.33. Adding the override there cause the following error.

> scheduler_perf.go:1160: failed to set versions (emu=1.33, min=1.32) during test: cannot set feature gate DRAConsumableCapacity to false, feature is PreAlpha at emulated version 1.33, min compatibility version 1.32

from

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/ec2c7739bc3ccc602f7af67b096d44a9f446122a/test/integration/scheduler_perf/scheduler_perf.go#L1156

Meanwhile, not adding that also cause the following error.

title: DRA: integration-test: cannot set PreAlpha feature gate at emulated version 1.33 but no allocator available for feature set (stable allocator)
text:
> 2026-02-13T00:51:17.1666700Z E0213 00:41:32.160846   27097 schedule_one.go:163] "Error selecting node for pod" err="running PreFilter plugin \"DynamicResources\": internal error: no allocator available for feature set {AdminAccess:false ConsumableCapacity:true DeviceBindingAndStatus:false DeviceTaints:false PartitionableDevices:false PrioritizedList:false}, enabled allocators: stable" pod="test/test-drarhn2d"

from

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/ec2c7739bc3ccc602f7af67b096d44a9f446122a/staging/src/k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation/structured/allocator.go#L171

The workaround that I did in my PR to have the test pass is to export allocatorEnabled function and add a similar logic to disable beta feature when allocator is`stable` and version >= 1.34 after emulation set.

title: DRA: integration-test: cannot set PreAlpha feature gate at emulated version 1.33 but no allocator available for feature set (stable allocator)
text:
```diff
@@ -1160,6 +1161,15 @@ func setupTestCase(t testing.TB, tc *testCase, featureGates map[featuregate.Feat
                }
                featureGates[features.OpportunisticBatching] = false
        }
+
+       // Disable DRAConsumableCapacity for the stable allocator when emulating v1.34+.
+       // At v1.33, the feature doesn't exist (PreAlpha) so we can't set it, but it's
+       // effectively disabled, which is what the stable allocator expects.
+       if structured.AllocatorEnabled("stable") &&
+               utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate.(featuregate.MutableVersionedFeatureGate).EmulationVersion().AtLeast(version.MustParse("1.34")) {
+               featuregatetesting.SetFeatureGateDuringTest(t, utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate, features.DRAConsumableCapacity, false)
+       }
+
        featuregatetesting.SetFeatureGatesDuringTest(t, utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate, featureGates)
```
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136611/changes#diff-a09bf733e7a7273560beb3d657d078a3bec7ba83baa4c

title: ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold
text: ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold

title: ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold
ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold
text:
```go
len(pod.Spec.InitContainers) + len(pod.Spec.Containers)
```
calculatePriority then uses this count to derive the max threshold:

```go
maxThreshold := maxContainerThreshold * int64(numContainers)
```

This means ImageVolume images increase the raw image score, but do not increase the max threshold. As a result, Pods using ImageVolume can receive a higher ImageLocality score, or reach MaxNodeScore more easily, because ImageVolume images are counted in the numerator but not in the threshold.

title: ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

I think this is a bug because, from the perspective of ImageLocality scoring, a container image and an ImageVolume image have the same locality effect: both are images required by the Pod, and both can avoid image pulling cost if they already exist on the node.

Therefore, ImageVolume images should be counted consistently in ImageLocality scoring.

Since #130231 made ImageVolume images contribute to sumImageScores, they should also contribute to the image count passed to calculatePriority.

The image count should be based on:

```go
len(pod.Spec.InitContainers) + len(pod.Spec.Containers) + numberOfImageVolumes
```

rather than only:

```go
len(pod.Spec.InitContainers) + len(pod.Spec.Containers)
```

With this behavior, a Pod with one regular container image plus one ImageVolume image would be scored consistently with a Pod that has the same two images as regular container images.

title: ImageLocality counts ImageVolume images in raw score but not in max threshold

title: scheduler handleSchedulingFailure: DATA RACE
text: Oh, so `-race` isn't used in CIs? (why?)

title: scheduler handleSchedulingFailure: DATA RACE
text:
In the past, `-race` caused performance issues in pull-kubernetes-integration. It works better nowadays, but still causes known flakes which makes it unsuitable for that job.

We have https://testgrid.k8s.io/sig-testing-canaries#integration-race-master now.

title: kube-apiserver removes all managedFields if conversion webhook is not available
text: kube-apiserver removes all managedFields if conversion webhook is not available

title: kube-apiserver removes all managedFields if conversion webhook is not available
text:
### What happened?

The apiserver removes all managedFields of an object under the following circumstances:
* object has managedField entries of multiple apiVersions
* conversion webhook is (potentially temporarily) not available/reachable
* there's a write on the object (via label, or client-side apply, SSA just returns an appropriate error, more details below)

title: kube-apiserver removes all managedFields if conversion webhook is not available
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

I would expect to get an error back from the apiserver in cases like this.
Removing all managedFields can have a big impact on the results of subsequent writes.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Required files:
[cr_cluster.yaml](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25218546/cr_cluster.yaml)

Create a kind cluster (I was using kindest/node:v1.35.0)
```bash
kind create cluster
```

Deploy the CRD (without conversion configured for now so we are able to prepare managedFields with v1beta1 & v1beta2)
```bash
kubectl apply -f ./crd_cluster_no_conversion.yaml

title: kube-apiserver removes all managedFields if conversion webhook is not available
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
Deploy and prepare the cluster
```bash
kubectl apply -f ./cr_cluster.yaml
kubectl label clusters.v1beta1.cluster.x-k8s.io cluster-1  def=ghi --overwrite
kubectl label clusters.v1beta2.cluster.x-k8s.io cluster-1  abc=def --overwrite
kubectl get cluster cluster-1 -o json --show-managed-fields | jq '.metadata.managedFields' | grep apiVersion
```

Update the CRD to configure an unreachable conversion webhook
```bash
kubectl apply -f ./crd_cluster.yaml
```

Try to update the cluster with SSA:
```
kubectl apply -f ./cr_cluster_ssa.yaml --server-side --field-manager=kubectl-ssa-test --show-managed-fields -o yaml
```
Output
```
Error from server: conversion webhook for cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta2, Kind=Cluster failed: Post "https://webhook-service.system.svc:443/convert?timeout=30s": service "webhook-service" not found
```

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
### What happened?

After #138430 re-gated the call to `setMemoryQoS` behind `utilfeature.DefaultFeatureGate.Enabled(kubefeatures.MemoryQoS)`, disabling the `MemoryQoS` alpha feature gate no longer clears stale `memory.min` / `memory.low` values from QoS-level cgroups (`kubepods.slice/kubepods-burstable.slice`, `kubepods.slice/kubepods-guaranteed.slice`, etc.) on cgroup v2 unified systems.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
Before #138430, `setMemoryQoS` was invoked unconditionally from `UpdateCgroups` on cgroup v2 unified mode. Its zero-out branch at [`pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308`](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308) resets `memory.min` / `memory.low` to 0 whenever `memoryReservationPolicy != TieredReservation`, which covers the "feature disabled or policy = None" case. With the gate check added at [L358](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L358), that zero-out branch is unreachable whenever the gate is off.

This is the QoS-level analogue of the pod-level regression that #137674 / #137726 already address. #137726 added `ReconcilePodMemoryMin` on `podContainerManagerImpl` but does not touch QoS-level cgroups, so the stale values at the QoS level remain indefinitely on any node that previously ran with the alpha gate enabled and was later rolled back.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

When MemoryQoS is disabled (feature gate off, or `memoryReservationPolicy = None`), QoS-level `memory.min` / `memory.low` should be reset to 0 on the next `UpdateCgroups` cycle. This matches the explicit assumption in #137674 that "QoS-level cgroups are correctly reset via the periodic `setMemoryQoS()` loop."

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Cgroup v2 unified node.
2. Start kubelet with `--feature-gates=MemoryQoS=true` and `memoryReservationPolicy: HardReservation`.
3. Create a few Burstable pods so that QoS-level values are populated. Verify:
   `cat /sys/fs/cgroup/kubepods.slice/kubepods-burstable.slice/memory.low` → non-zero.
4. Disable the gate by restarting kubelet with `--feature-gates=MemoryQoS=false` (or set `memoryReservationPolicy: None`).
5. After the next `UpdateCgroups` cycle, observe that `memory.low` still carries the previous non-zero value. Restart kubelet and repeat — the value persists.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

- Closely related: #137674 (pod-level tracking issue), #137726 (pod-level fix), #137889 (earlier removal of `reconcilePodMemoryProtection`), #138431 (motivated #138430).
- The pod-level fix in #137726 does not cover QoS-level cgroups; there is no existing analogue on `qosContainerManagerImpl`.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
### Proposed fix options

There are two viable approaches; I would appreciate guidance from sig-node on which shape is preferred:

1. **Keep `setMemoryQoS` unconditional** on cgroup v2 unified mode inside `UpdateCgroups`, and move the feature-gate check *inside* `setMemoryQoS` so it still runs the zero-out branch (L303–L308) but skips the populate branch. Minimal diff; preserves the original cleanup contract.
2. **Add a QoS-level reconcile method** analogous to `ReconcilePodMemoryMin` (#137726) that walks QoS cgroups and zeroes `memory.min` / `memory.low` when the gate is off. Larger diff but symmetric with the pod-level structure.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
### Kubernetes version

master at `03779bbd0042` and any release branch that carries the #138430 backports.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text: In any case, we should not be adding new ungated behavior into 1.36 related to Alpha features that impacts clusters that never opted into the alpha.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
> Before https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/138430, setMemoryQoS was invoked unconditionally from UpdateCgroups on cgroup v2 unified mode.

Yes. That was a regression from 1.35 behavior with undesirable side effects on cpu cgroups and modifying cluster behavior for node which hadn't opted into the alpha feature.

> Its zero-out branch at [pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308) resets memory.min / memory.low to 0 whenever memoryReservationPolicy != TieredReservation, which covers the "feature disabled or policy = None" case. With the gate check added at [L358](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L358), that zero-out branch is unreachable whenever the gate is off.

My understanding is that this matches 1.35 behavior, right?

text:

to check my understanding that master matches 1.35 behavior here, and about the setting still happening once at kubelet startup

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
> Before https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/138430, setMemoryQoS was invoked unconditionally from UpdateCgroups on cgroup v2 unified mode.

#138430 restored behavior consistent with 1.35:

1.35:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/31d47ca373f3f1b0e796a24eeefcfec8087a9408/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L345-L349

1.36 / master:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b139cc796075033fcc6bca80a39f09d06503ca4a/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L356-L360

It would be helpful to clarify whether this issue is reporting a behavior change relative to 1.35 or not.

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
> > Before [#138430](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/138430), setMemoryQoS was invoked unconditionally from UpdateCgroups on cgroup v2 unified mode.
> 
> Yes. That was a regression from 1.35 behavior with undesirable side effects on cpu cgroups and modifying cluster behavior for node which hadn't opted into the alpha feature.
> 
> > Its zero-out branch at [pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L303-L308) resets memory.min / memory.low to 0 whenever memoryReservationPolicy != TieredReservation, which covers the "feature disabled or policy = None" case. With the gate check added at [L358](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/kubelet/cm/qos_container_manager_linux.go#L358), that zero-out branch is unreachable whenever the gate is off.
> 
> My understanding is that this matches 1.35 behavior, right?

title: kubelet: QoS-level memory.min / memory.low is not cleared after MemoryQoS feature gate is disabled (regression from #138430)
text:
@liggitt is right that master matches 1.35 behavior and the gate check was the same in 1.35. So this isn't a regression from #138430. That said, the KEP [promises](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-node/2570-memory-qos/README.md#can-the-feature-be-disabled-once-it-has-been-enabled-ie-can-we-roll-back-the-enablement) that disabling the feature resets memory.min/memory.high to default values. That part had not actually been implemented in the old behavior. PR #137719 (v1.36) was the first attempt to deliver it (ungating setMemoryQoS), but it caused the systemd `cpu.max` side effect that motivated #138430.

Regarding addressing this gap, both proposed options risk routing cleanup back through the same systemd path that caused the side effects. I'd like to take a bit more time to think through the right mechanism before recommending an implementation.

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
### What happened?

After upgrading a Talos Kubernetes cluster from v1.35 to v1.36, some running pods stopped receiving projected service account token rotations. The affected pods were not terminating.

During the control-plane restart window, kubelet logged transient TokenRequest / NodeAuthorizer failures like:

`Error preparing data for projected volume sa-token for pod network/external-5f8b6fdd5c-g4vzm: failed to fetch token: serviceaccounts "external" is forbidden: User "system:node:k8s-1" cannot create resource "serviceaccounts/token" in API group "" in the namespace "network": no relationship found between node 'k8s-1' and this object`

There were also kubelet TokenRequest EOFs against the local API endpoint:

`Couldn't update token: Post "https://127.0.0.1:7445/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/serviceaccounts/default/token": EOF`

After the API server became healthy again, the projected token files stayed stale for hours. Example affected pod:

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
pod: network/external-5f8b6fdd5c-g4vzm
node: k8s-1
token iat: 2026-04-28T20:46:41Z
token exp: 2026-04-28T21:46:41Z
projected volume symlink stayed at: ..2026_04_28_20_46_41...

A second pod on another node had a token issued at 2026-04-28T20:52:02Z, expiring at 2026-04-28T21:52:02Z, and also remained frozen at that timestamp.

The application impact was Envoy Gateway data-plane pods failing xDS authentication after token expiry:

`failed to validate token {"error": "token review found error: [invalid bearer token, service account token has expired]"}`

Because the Envoy data-plane could not reconnect to xDS, it kept stale endpoint config until the pods were restarted.

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

For running pods, kubelet should keep retrying projected service account token refresh after transient TokenRequest/API server/NodeAuthorizer failures.

A temporary failure during apiserver restart should not leave the projected token file permanently stale or expired. Once the apiserver/NodeAuthorizer state is healthy again, kubelet should recover token rotation without
requiring the pod to be restarted.

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

I do not yet have a minimal deterministic reproducer, but the observed incident was:

1. Run a pod with a projected service account token, for example expirationSeconds: 3600.
2. Restart or upgrade the control plane while kubelet is refreshing projected tokens.
3. During the restart, kubelet receives TokenRequest failures such as:
   serviceaccounts "<name>" is forbidden ... no relationship found between node '<node>' and this object
   or EOFs from the API endpoint.
4. Wait past the projected token expiration.
5. Inspect the projected token file/symlink inside the pod or from the host.
6. Observe that the token remains expired and the projected volume timestamp does not advance, even after the API server is healthy again.
7. Restarting the pod causes a fresh token to be mounted and resolves the issue.

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

This does not appear to be an application-level token caching issue. In the observed case, Envoy’s SDS secret last-updated timestamp matched the projected token file timestamp. The file itself stopped changing.

This also seems different from:

- #116481, which involves terminating pods/preStop hooks.
- #130442, which involved PVCs in terminating state.

The affected pods here were ordinary running pods.

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
This has recurred on a stable Kubernetes v1.36.0 cluster.

The cluster was upgraded from Kubernetes v1.35.4 to v1.36.0 at `2026-04-28T20:46:23Z`.

Relevant affected pod spec:

```yaml
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  serviceAccountName: internal # also observed with external
  containers:
  - name: envoy
    volumeMounts:
    - name: sa-token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/token
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: sa-token
    projected:
      defaultMode: 420
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          audience: envoy-gateway.network.svc.cluster.local
          expirationSeconds: 3600
          path: sa-token
```

This is an explicit projected service account token, not the default automounted kube-api-access-* token.

Logs were searched from 2026-04-18T00:00:00Z to the v1.36.0 upgrade at 2026-04-28T20:46:23Z, while the cluster
was on v1.35.4. There were zero matches for:

- failed to validate token
- service account token has expired

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
The first matching post-upgrade event found was at 2026-04-29T07:25:36.644Z:

`failed to validate token {"error": "token review found error: [invalid bearer token, service account token has
expired]"}`

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/3cf3d54eaa58738efa283b2273a4dd24c8fd6766/pkg/kubelet/token/token_manager.go#L96-L103 is the code in question

The kubelet doesn't cache token fetch errors... it will retry the next time it is asked by the storage subsystem (which happens on a loop every few seconds, I think) if the token it last mounted is >=80% of its lifetime.

(There were also no changes between 1.35 and 1.36 in this area, so I don't think the upgrade was relevant)

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text:
I bumped into this also. My observations:
 - apiserver audit log shows token requests from kubelet at expected intervals
 - all broken pods were started before kubelet restart

I am pretty sure it is Talos bug caused by using bind instead of rbind for mounting /var/lib/kubelet into kubelet container. Reported here: https://github.com/siderolabs/talos/issues/13352

title: Kubelet can leave projected service account token expired after transient TokenRequest/NodeAuthorizer failures during control-plane restart
text: I think this is a Talos problem so im happy to close this unless we think Kube could have provided better error messages or something here

text: At the interval kubelet will attempt these, I think increasing logging would be really spammy and possibly problematic

title: After switching the Go version to 1.26.1, the binary package has become significantly larger.
text: After switching the Go version to 1.26.1, the binary package has become significantly larger.

title: After switching the Go version to 1.26.1, the binary package has become significantly larger.
text:
### What happened?

The Kubernetes 1.34.1 code is used to compile a binary file using Go 1.24.6. The compilation command is as follows:
go build -ldflags "-s -w" -tags "grpcnotrace" -o _output/bin/kube-apiserver cmd/kube-apiserver/apiserver.go
Then, the binary file is compiled using Go 1.26.1. It is found that the size of the kube-apiserver binary file compiled using Go 1.24.6 is 85233956,
while the size of the kube-apiserver binary file compiled using Go 1.26.1 is 103235849, which is 18 MB larger than that of the binary file compiled using Go 1.24.6. This is unreasonable.
In addition, after I removed -tags "grpcnotrace", the size of the binary file compiled using Go 1.24.6 was 102572324, and the size of the binary file compiled using Go 1.26.1 was 103534857. That is, the -tags "grpcnotrace" option was very effective in Go 1.24.6, but had almost no effect in Go 1.26.1.

title: After switching the Go version to 1.26.1, the binary package has become significantly larger.
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

The effect of -tags "grpcnotrace" should be the same in Go 1.26.1 and Go 1.24.6, and the binary size should not change significantly.

title: After switching the Go version to 1.26.1, the binary package has become significantly larger.
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Run the go build -ldflags "-s -w" -tags "grpcnotrace" -o _output/bin/kube-apiserver cmd/kube-apiserver/apiserver.go command to compile the binary file in go1.24.6 and go1.26.1, and then compare the sizes of the two binary files.

title: Kubelet retries NodeStageVolume indefinitely on NOT_FOUND
text: Kubelet retries NodeStageVolume indefinitely on NOT_FOUND

title: Kubelet retries NodeStageVolume indefinitely on NOT_FOUND
text:
### What happened?

When a CSI driver's `NodeStageVolume` returns `NOT_FOUND` (gRPC code 5), the kubelet continuously retries the operation with exponential backoff. However, the kubelet does not perform the recovery behavior specified in the CSI spec for this error code.

This results in pods being stuck in `ContainerCreating` state indefinitely, requiring manual intervention.

title: Kubelet retries NodeStageVolume indefinitely on NOT_FOUND
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

According to the [CSI spec for NodeStageVolume errors](https://github.com/container-storage-interface/spec/blob/master/spec.md#nodestagevolume-errors), when `NOT_FOUND` is returned:

> **Condition:** Volume does not exist.
>
> **gRPC Code:** `5 NOT_FOUND`
>
> **Recovery Behavior:** "Caller MUST verify that the `volume_id` is correct and that the volume is accessible and has not been deleted before retrying with exponential back off."

The kubelet should follow this recovery behavior: **verify that the volume is accessible** before retrying.

Currently, the kubelet only retries `NodeStageVolume` without any verification step, which does not comply with the CSI spec.

title: Kubelet retries NodeStageVolume indefinitely on NOT_FOUND
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Deploy a CSI driver that returns `NOT_FOUND` from `NodeStageVolume` when the volume device is not present on the node
2. Create a PVC and a Pod that mounts it
3. Cause the volume to become inaccessible (e.g., detach via cloud API, node reboot, storage issue)
4. Observe:
   - Pod stuck in `ContainerCreating`
   - Kubelet retries `NodeStageVolume` indefinitely
   - No verification of volume accessibility as specified by CSI spec

title: kube-apiserver problem
text: Is this `context deadline exceeded` warning from kube-apiserver’s etcd client in v1.34.x expected under etcd latency/load, or does it indicate a regression or bug in retry/timeout handling introduced recently?

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
> Is this `context deadline exceeded` warning from kube-apiserver’s etcd client in v1.34.x expected under etcd latency/load, or does it indicate a regression or bug in retry/timeout handling introduced recently?

Actually, this bug exists in several versions

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
Before proposing a code change, I’d like to confirm the intended behavior.
While looking into this, I noticed that the etcd client retry logic treats all **DeadlineExceeded** errors as non-retryable context failures, which results in no retries **(attempt: 0)** even for repeatable/immutable RPCs like **Range()**.

Is this behavior intentional by design, or should **DeadlineExceeded** be considered retryable when the request is idempotent and the context still has remaining time?

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
> Before proposing a code change, I’d like to confirm the intended behavior. While looking into this, I noticed that the etcd client retry logic treats all **DeadlineExceeded** errors as non-retryable context failures, which results in no retries **(attempt: 0)** even for repeatable/immutable RPCs like **Range()**.
> 
> Is this behavior intentional by design, or should **DeadlineExceeded** be considered retryable when the request is idempotent and the context still has remaining time?

Actually, this error is reported by the kube-apiserver client. I think we need to optimize the logic of kube-apiserver to connect to the etcd cluster, or disable this log alert

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
> > Before proposing a code change, I’d like to confirm the intended behavior. While looking into this, I noticed that the etcd client retry logic treats all **DeadlineExceeded** errors as non-retryable context failures, which results in no retries **(attempt: 0)** even for repeatable/immutable RPCs like **Range()**.
> > Is this behavior intentional by design, or should **DeadlineExceeded** be considered retryable when the request is idempotent and the context still has remaining time?
> 
> Actually, this error is reported by the kube-apiserver client. I think we need to optimize the logic of kube-apiserver to connect to the etcd cluster, or disable this log alert

Thanks for the clarification. Understood that this is emitted from the kube-apiserver side rather than an etcd client bug.

title: kube-apiserver problem
text: I’ll take a look at the kube-apiserver etcd client logic (context/timeout handling and retry usage) to see whether this can be optimized, and also evaluate whether the WARN log is too aggressive for transient DeadlineExceeded cases. I’ll follow up with a concrete proposal before making any code changes.

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
**Report generated by [stackseek.io](https://stackseek.io/)**

QUICK SUMMARY:
We identified a communication timeout between the system's core management server and its internal database, which was causing intermittent errors like a phone call dropping due to a weak signal. We are adjusting the connection settings to make the system more resilient to high traffic.

ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS:
The error 'context deadline exceeded' in 'clientv3/retry_interceptor.go' indicates that the kube-apiserver is timing out while attempting to communicate with the etcd cluster. This typically happens when etcd is overloaded, experiencing slow disk I/O, or network latency is high, preventing the request from completing within the default timeout period. The issue manifests after running for a period of time, suggesting resource exhaustion or accumulating load.

ERROR LOCATION:
clientv3/retry_interceptor.go:62

EXECUTION PATH:
kube-apiserver -> etcd client -> retry_interceptor -> unary invoker -> context deadline exceeded

title: kube-apiserver problem
text:
RESOLUTION STEPS:
No action is required on your side. Our engineering team has been notified and is working on a fix. We will update you once resolved.

REPLICATION STEPS:
Run a Kubernetes cluster with version 1.34.3.
Generate high load on the apiserver (e.g., heavy object creation/deletion).
Observe kube-apiserver logs for 'rpc error: code = DeadlineExceeded' after a period of sustained load.

SUGGESTED FIX:
Increase the connection timeout configuration for the etcd client. In the kube-apiserver startup arguments, add or update the flag: `--etcd-request-timeout=10s` (default is typically lower). Additionally, verify etcd disk performance meets requirements (low fsync latency).

GENERATED TEST CODE (GO):
package main

import (
	"context"
	"errors"
	"testing"
	"time"
)

// Simulate the specific gRPC error format returned by etcd client
var ErrRPCTimeout = errors.New("rpc error: code = DeadlineExceeded desc = context deadline exceeded")

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
text: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

I would like to propose adding net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the list of "safe" sysctls in Kubernetes.

Both of these sysctls are namespaced in the Linux kernel:

tcp_slow_start_after_idle has been namespaced since Linux 3.11.

tcp_notsent_lowat has been namespaced since Linux 3.12.

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
text:
### Why is this needed?

As with the recently accepted change for TCP read/write buffers https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/125234, modern high-performance applications require fine-grained control over the networking stack that cannot always be applied globally at the node level.

1. net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle for long-lived connections that have idle periods (like database pools or persistent API streams), the default behavior is to reset the congestion window after an idle period. Disabling this (0) is important for applications that need to maintain high throughput immediately upon resuming transmission without waiting for a slow-start ramp-up.

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
text:
2. net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat This is important for HTTP/2 and gRPC services. It limits the amount of unsent data in the write queue, which significantly reduces "bufferbloat" and improves responsiveness/latency for real-time streams. Setting this per-pod allows latency-critical microservices to coexist on the same node as throughput-oriented bulk transfer services.

Allowing these as safe sysctls enables developers to optimize their application's networking behavior via the Pod securityContext without requiring administrative intervention to modify Kubelet configuration or host-level sysctls.

Note: Text was formatted and edited with Google Gemini.

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
text:
AFAIK the criteria for a sysctl to be 'safe' is not just that it is namespaced, that indeed required, but also that its resource usage is isolated and accounted for, ensuring no impact on the host. (See related discussion: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/62334#issuecomment-2671243412)

In this case, both seems to satisfy those requirements:

- net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle affects only the logic of the TCP stack (no resource impact).

- net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat affects memory allocation for buffers, but this memory is correctly charged to the container's Memory Cgroup per https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/125234

So technically this LGTM , I'm more fuzzy about the mechanics of the kubelet to implement this

title: Add net.ipv4.tcp_slow_start_after_idle and net.ipv4.tcp_notsent_lowat to the safe sysctl list
text:
> I'm more fuzzy about the mechanics of the kubelet to implement this

Indeed, kubelet only maintained a safe list(some may also consider kernel version for kernel updates). No special logic IIUC. 
Generally, container runtime will trust kubelet(CRI call) to set all sysctls for the pod.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

When re-creating the pod that was killed, it should be able to start up again since there is enough memory available on a single node to satisfy the memory and CPU requests.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Set the memory manager policy to "Static" and topology manager policy to "restricted".   On a two-NUMA-node worker node create a smallish Pod in the Guaranteed QoS class that easily fits on one NUMA node worth of memory.  Create a second Pod in the Guaranteed QoS class with a big enough memory request that it cannot fit on one NUMA node.  Delete the first Pod.  After the memory freed up by the Pod deletion shows as available, attempt to recreate the first Pod.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
I'm unable to test against current master.  I did spin up a VM to test 1.31.2 via minikube and I think I might have uncovered a new and different bug.

I had 8GB of allocatable memory on each of two NUMA nodes.  Key kubelet args were:

`-cpu-manager-policy=static --kube-reserved=memory=1Gi --memory-manager-policy=Static --reserved-cpus=0,4 --reserved-memory=0:memory=1Gi;1:memory=1Gi --system-reserved=memory=1Gi --topology-manager-policy=restricted`

I was able to create the first pod with 1cpu and 256Mi of memory, but when I tried to create the second pod with 1cpu and 9Gi of memory (to force it to allocate memory from both NUMA nodes) it errored out unexpectedly:

```
cfriesen@debian:~$ minikube kubectl -- get pod kube-mgrr-2 -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  annotations:

      {"apiVersion":"v1","kind":"Pod","metadata":{"annotations":{},"name":"kube-mgrr-2","namespace":"default"},"spec":{"containers":[{"image":"gcr.io/kubernet

  name: kube-mgrr-2

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
Based on the discussion at https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/123443 I think the original test setup here is actually invalid, since it has two pods, one of which is single-NUMA-node and one of which is cross-NUMA-node.

It seems like it should be possible in the testcase I describe to initially create the smaller Pod as a multi-NUMA-node Pod even though its resources could be provided by a single NUMA node.  That way we wouldn't artificially prevent the second larger Pod from being scheduled even though there is actually enough memory available for it.

I guess this issue is that we have to decide between preferring NUMA isolation or preferring allowing as many Pods as possible to run on a node even if their NUMA allocations aren't ideal.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
This seems to raise the interesting prospect of order-dependent Pod scheduling, where if we schedule a larger cross-NUMA-node Pod first and then a smaller Pod second it might allow both to be scheduled (with the smaller also configured as cross-NUMA-node).

But if the smaller one is scheduled first as a single NUMA-node Pod it would prevent the larger cross-NUMA-node Pod from being scheduled.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
> This seems to raise the interesting prospect of order-dependent Pod scheduling, where if we schedule a larger cross-NUMA-node Pod first and then a smaller Pod second it might allow both to be scheduled (with the smaller also configured as cross-NUMA-node).
> 
> But if the smaller one is scheduled first as a single NUMA-node Pod it would prevent the larger cross-NUMA-node Pod from being scheduled.

Yes I agree. The rational behind it (capture in the Memory Manager's KEP) is that if one node is used for single NUMA-node allocation, somewhere else in the cluster there is a node that could use for a cross-NUMA-node allocations

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
One problem with that rationale is that it basically means that you can't configure the NUMA-aware memory manager and a Topology Manager policy of "restricted" on a small K8s cluster where there may not be a free worker node available for cross-NUMA-node allocations.   We have quite a few single-K8s-node clusters out at the edge, for example.   So we either need to use "best-effort" Topology Manager policy or else turn off the NUMA-aware memory manager.

It'd be nice if we had a way to tell K8s how much memory a pod is allowed to allocate from each NUMA node, but I don't think Linux gives us that level of control via cgroups and numactl.

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text: I'd be open and very interested in changing the behavior of memory manager, but this warrants a KEP change with the related process

title: NUMA-aware memory manager and Topology Manager policy of "restricted" results in TopologyAffinityError when it shouldn't
text:
Please check my [comment](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/128708#issuecomment-2489591274) on the second issue why mixing of single and multi numa allocation is problematic.

I don't think that KEP changes is enough, because right now it's a kernel limitation.

title: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.
text: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.

title: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.
text:
The `apiserver_response_sizes` correctly report `GET` and `LIST` metrics for all resources. However, for `WATCH`, its only present for built in resources. This means resources defined in CustomResourceDefinitions are not present.

Its not 100% clear to me what the expected behavior was when it was introduced (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/49117), and if the plan was to not have `watch` metrics here - but we have seen it useful in practice. The verb definition here has changed a bit as well, and https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/93523 + https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/81660 split the definition of `verb` and `reportedVerb`. And given this metric is treated as STABLE now, it feels wrong to me to remove the existing `WATCH` metrics here.

Here is an example of how it works today;

```bash
$ kubectl get --raw /metrics | egrep 'apiserver_response_sizes_sum\{component="apiserver",group="(cilium.io)?",resource="(pods|ciliumnetworkpolicies)",scope="cluster"'
apiserver

title: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.
text:
_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="",resource="pods",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="LIST",version="v1"} 1.00624648e+08
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="",resource="pods",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="WATCH",version="v1"} 2.6141637319e+10
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="cilium.io",resource="ciliumnetworkpolicies",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="LIST",version="v2"} 1.723691e+06
```

As seen both resources have `LIST` metrics, but only the built in resource `pods` has `WATCH`.

title: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

I would expect the above request to also contain WATCH for the custom type as well;
```bash
$ kubectl get --raw /metrics | egrep 'apiserver_response_sizes_sum\{component="apiserver",group="(cilium.io)?",resource="(pods|ciliumnetworkpolicies)",scope="cluster"'
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="",resource="pods",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="LIST",version="v1"} 1.00624648e+08
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="",resource="pods",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="WATCH",version="v1"} 2.6141637319e+10
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="cilium.io",resource="ciliumnetworkpolicies",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="LIST",version="v2"} 1.723691e+06
apiserver_response_sizes_sum{component="apiserver",group="cilium.io",resource="ciliumnetworkpolicies",scope="cluster",subresource="",verb="WATCH",version="v2"} 1.090217513e+09
```

title: response_sizes metrics for verb=WATCH is only present for built in resources.
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Install a CRD, have at least one watcher on it, and use `kubectl get --raw /metrics | grep 'apiserver_response_sizes_sum'` and look for the `WATCH` metric of that resource.

title: DRA extended resource: decide about metrics name
text: DRA extended resource: decide about metrics name

title: DRA extended resource: decide about metrics name
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

From https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134523#discussion_r2480322120:

Do we want to distinguish between `Subsystem:      SchedulerSubsystem,` (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b31119d205a839aab40b2d819a58d4fabacd9b47/pkg/scheduler/metrics/metrics.go#L466-L473) and `Subsystem:      ResourceClaimSubsystem,` (over in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b4d4cc93840fa30a305b013acd1b1060ed3f8ee2/pkg/controller/resourceclaim/metrics/metrics.go#L26-L41)?

We could define a shared metric with subsystem "dynamic_resource_allocation", name "resourceclaim_creates_total". Then users can aggregated that metric across different components (scheduler, kube-controller-manager) or look at those in isolation by filtering by component, depending on what they are interested in.

Manually aggregating the two differently named metrices was mentioned as an alternative.

/sig instrumentation
/wg device-management
cc @yliaog @bitoku

title: DRA extended resource: decide about metrics name
text:
### Why is this needed?

We postponed the decision for 1.35, but should collect more feedback or try it out. We can still change it before graduating these metrics to beta.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning
text: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning
text:
### What happened?

Occasionally on newer Windows Server 2019 OS builds, Windows Pods experience connection timeouts when attempting to access a Kubernetes LoadBalancer VIP that routes back to a pod on the exact same node (hairpinning).
When this issue happened, the `NatPool` for the LoadBalancer policy is incorrectly set to the VIP itself instead of the Node's IP address. Because the backend pod sees the VIP as the source IP, it sends its reply back to the VIP. The Windows virtual switch (HNS) intercepts this reply, treats it as a brand-new inbound LoadBalancer request, and routes it into an infinite loop (or drops it), causing the connection to time out.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

Pod-to-LB hairpin traffic should always route correctly via a NatPool built with the Node IP.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

It occurs occasionally, so hard to reproduce consistently. 
1. Deploy a LoadBalancer service with local endpoints.
2. Run hnsdiag list loadbalancers on the host. Observe the NatPool uses the External VIP instead of the Node IP because "SourceVip" is "".

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy: Empty sourceVip causes broken NatPool occasionally, breaking LoadBalancer hairpinning
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

1. Empty `sourceVip` breaks hairpin routing on newer Windows HNS kernels.
In `pkg/proxy/windows/proxier.go`, `sourceVip` is an empty string "".
```
var sourceVip = proxier.sourceVip
if containsPublicIP || containsNodeIP {
sourceVip = proxier.nodeIP.String()
}
```
When `kube-proxy` passes "" to the Windows Host Compute Network (HCN) API:
* Sometimes (older versions) Windows HNS defaulted an empty SourceVIP to the Node IP. Hairpinning worked.
* Occationally Windows HNS default an empty SourceVIP to the Frontend VIP. Hairpinning breaks due to the routing loop.

title: Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation
text:
Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation

The current CronJob documentation explains the concept well, but lacks a real-world use case that helps beginners understand when to use it.

Suggestion:
Add a practical example such as:
- Scheduled data processing job
- Daily batch cleanup job
- Periodic report generation

Also clarify how CronJob differs from Job in practical scenarios.

I can work on adding this example if this approach is acceptable.

title: Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation
text:
/sig apps
/kind documentation

> [...] but lacks a real-world use case that helps beginners understand when to use it.

https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/controllers/cron-jobs/

This is the third sentence in the page:

> CronJob is meant for performing regular scheduled actions such as backups, report generation, and so on [...]

If you mean we should put a "real report generator" then no, we don't do that because it wouldn't be generic and we'd have to keep the image up to date etc. The examples are meant to illustrate how the APIs work, not to be production workloads, that's better maintained in a project for that workload.

> Also clarify how CronJob differs from Job in practical scenarios.

What else do you expect to outline, exactly?

title: Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation
text:
Hey @BenTheElder !
I am starting to work on this issue, can you please assign it to me? Thanks in advance.

title: Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation
text:
Thanks for the clarification.

I agree that production-style workloads would not be appropriate for the documentation examples, and I did not intend to propose a workload-specific implementation.

What I was trying to suggest was a smaller documentation improvement focused on beginner understanding. For example, adding a short clarification around practical usage boundaries between Job and CronJob, such as:

* Job → one-time or finite task execution
* CronJob → scheduled repeated Job creation

I noticed new users sometimes confuse whether CronJob directly replaces long-running workloads or whether it is simply a scheduler around Jobs.

If that distinction is already sufficiently covered elsewhere in the docs, I’m happy to close this out. Otherwise, I can propose a very small wording improvement or cross-reference update instead.

title: Add real-world data pipeline example to CronJob documentation
text:
> I am starting to work on this issue, can you please assign it to me? Thanks in advance.

This is still needs-triage and doesn't have consensus yet. Any anyhow this is the wrong repo for the documentation (that is kubernetes/website).

> I noticed new users sometimes confuse whether CronJob directly replaces long-running workloads or whether it is simply a scheduler around Jobs.

ACK, if you think that needs further clarification in the docs, please file an issue with kubernetes/website.

### These PRs optimize the performance of EventedPLEG and resolve certain bugs, such as multiple starts of the same container:

- [ ]  #130893: Optimize conditions for IPPVS to trigger sync pods
- [ ]  #131024: Fix pod resizing is too slow when using evented PLEG (it depends on #130893 to function properly)
- [ ]  #129355: Using real-time container events for pod state determination (fixes bugs and improves the speed of pod state transitions, it depends on #130893 and #131024  to function properly)

title: [Umbrella] Tracking EventedPLEG Issues in v1.35
text:

- [x] https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/pull/35836: Add a containerd-based node e2e test job for EventedPLEG(This PR should be prioritized for merging to ensure that the code changes made during the v1.35 cycle remain fully compatible with containerd.)
- [ ] Many existing tests currently require EventedPLEG to be disabled to run properly. We need to enable it for these tests to demonstrate the effectiveness of our changes made in v1.35.
- [ ] Add periodic test jobs for both node-e2e and e2e tests to ensure this feature functions correctly.

Issues we still need to discuss:

title: kube-scheduler panics for DRA `allocationMode: All` when a matching device consumes counters
text:
### What happened?

kube-scheduler panics when filtering a Pod that references a DRA ResourceClaim if:

- The ResourceClaim request uses `allocationMode: All`.
- A matching device has `consumesCounters`.
- The same ResourceSlice pool has `sharedCounters`.

text:
### What did you expect to happen?

kube-scheduler should not panic. It should either allocate the claim or report a normal scheduling failure/error for the Pod.

title: kube-scheduler panics for DRA `allocationMode: All` when a matching device consumes counters
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

This can be reproduced in a default single-node kind cluster. No DRA driver is required.

```bash
kind build node-image v1.36.0
kind create cluster --image kindest/node:v1.36.0
```

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
Block new `Disable*` feature gates

Whilst working on issue https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/70585 and https://github.com/kubernetes/website/pull/53535 I formed a view that we should avoid having feature gates can have names that begin with "Disable".
We could add a CI/CD check that automatically points this out, because without automation it's just a guideline that people might now even have heard about.

That's my opinion, anyway. I hope this issue gets people agreeing with it.

Here's a sample of some Markdown that might help you see why the "Disable" prefix is harder to work with:

> When the feature gate `DisableCPUQuotaWithExclusiveCPUs` is enabled (the default), then Kubernetes
> does **not** enforce CPU quota for Pods that use the [Guaranteed](https://k8s.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/pod-qos/#guaranteed) QoS class.
> 
> You can disable the `DisableCPUQuotaWithExclusiveCPUs` feature gate to restore the legacy behavior.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
Basically, the double negatives are uncomfortable to explain, tricky to learn, and they are not fun to localize either (in some languages, a double negative is still a negative; in others, it's like code and it's a positive).

The obvious fix is to rename the feature gate and invert the sense, eg to have a feature gate that is named `LegacyCPUQuotaEnforcementWithExclusiveCPUs`, and that is **off** by default. But we could really use some automation to point this out to people, rather than relying on (eg) SIG Architecture to point it out, or SIG Docs when people try to document it.

---

Before we make an automation change, let's update [`contributors/devel/sig-architecture/feature-gates.md`](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/82b8a1affb3376d0fa1e8f381d4847680bbbba41/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/feature-gates.md) in [k/community](https://github.com/kubernetes/community) to recommend that people don't start the names of gates with "Disable".

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
(and before _that_, we should wait for this issue to be triaged as accepted)

/sig architecture
/kind feature

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: I support the proposal, but as SIG Testing TL I need to point out that this is not something that SIG Testing owns. The approval for this proposal probably falls under SIG Architecture and the implementation of that check under SIG Architecture or API-Machinery. There's already `hack/verify-featuregates.sh` (mostly @siyuanfoundation).

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
/remove-sig testing
/sig architecture

We should exempt the feature gates we already have, then, and clearly explain why adding more exceptions is the wrong course.

SIG API Machinery might own feature gates - I haven't checked - but they are usually a cross-cutting concern.

Thanks @pohly.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
> The approval for this proposal probably falls under SIG Architecture and the implementation of that check under SIG Architecture or API-Machinery. There's already hack/verify-featuregates.sh

Nit: We have involvement in those hack scripts and approach to linting in general, but we already have a featuregate linter in place (I worked with Siyuan on this ...), so there's nothing additional for SIG Testing to do thin this case.

Regardless, I do think this is fundamentally a SIG Architecture decision first, but if we decide to do this there, then next with my SIG Testing hat I would just point to hack/verify-featuregates.sh and the underlying tooling.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: So which SIG owns `hack/verify-featuregates.sh`? I'm just genuinely curious. In the end we will still need a volunteer to work on this, regardless of the SIG.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: @pohly sig-arch takes care of things overall.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
SIG Arch owns the project-wide architecture discussion "what do we name featuregates".
SIG API-Machinery owns the featuregate tooling.
SIG Testing handles "make verify" and associated back scripts. This included reviewing introducing the featuregate linter.

This won't require a change to the script. This will require a minor change to the go code underneath it that vets featuregates. That part is straightforward and will be reviewed by OWNERS (regardless of "SIG") once the decision is made in SIG Arch yes/no on the concept.
Someone invested in the change can submit it once Arch discusses in concept (we should probably raise this at the meeting), and people don't necessarily map 1:1 to SIGs.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: I will try to make a SIG Architecture call to put this idea on the table formally. I really want it to be a soft reminder to reviewers rather than a thing that blocks a merge.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: (why only a soft reminder? well, for example, we might have agreed to use a particular feature gate name in an existing KEP, or - at a stretch - the feature gate name that triggers the warning is actually `DisabledJobsCountAgainstQuota` or something like that).

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text:
We don't have a great way to do soft-reminders, the problem is surfacing them.

We tried this with "linter-hints" but as far as I can tell most contributors are confused by the failures while a few persistent contributors understand the actual intent.

I would recommend a hard failure combined with an exception list if we do this. Then if a KEP already agreed to a gate and prefers to stick to it, it can just be approved into an exception list in the linter.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: Versus the golangci linter hints, there should be relatively few exceptions to make. We've had pretty good results with hard failure + exception list.

title: Block new `Disable*` feature gates
text: Ok, hard is easier to implement. Let's go for that.
