title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

The k8s client-go should make use of the upgradable websocket connection to watch for changes to resources, if the apiserver version allows it.

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text:
### Why is this needed?

Make use of modern http/2 compatible websockets instead of streaming chunked responses.

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text:
> The k8s client-go should make use of the upgradable websocket connection to watch for changes to resources, if the apiserver version allows it.

I'm not sure how supported is the websockets imlpementation of watch, @wojtek-t you may know?

I also think that the direction the project is going to improve watches is described in this kep https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-api-machinery/3157-watch-list

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text: Are there specific efficiency gains for websockets? The chunked impl is certainly simpler to reason about

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text: @liggitt: I think the problem is that many http clients (e.g. the rust http client (`ureq`)) doesn't return a response until it reaches an EOF.

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text: Also, chunked responses are deprecated in http/2.

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text:
> I think the problem is that many http clients (e.g. the rust http client (`ureq`)) doesn't return a response until it reaches an EOF.

I find that *very* surprising, and if accurate that seems like a significant gap in those clients... that means they are incapable of receiving streaming responses that won't fit in memory?

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text: We do have an implementation of a [websocket](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/95d71c464a6a5f81ae36bddc86972b3b2a1c40a5/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/handlers/watch.go#L179) based watch server already. The watch stream currently upgrades to websocket if the handshake headers are set. So, I'm assuming k8s already has plans to modernize the event streaming API.

title: Initiate websocket handshake to watch for events in cache.ListWatch
text:
> I'm assuming k8s already has plans to modernize the event streaming API.

That was actually put in place long prior to Kubernetes 1.0 - https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/493

There are no plans to change existing consumers to use the websocket version that I'm aware of.

See discussion in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/493#issuecomment-49338230 about why websocket was necessary / much easier for browser clients, but plain http streaming was simpler for clients that could support that.

title: An object created through SSA with a non-nil deletion timestamp will not get deleted
text: An object created through SSA with a non-nil deletion timestamp will not get deleted

title: An object created through SSA with a non-nil deletion timestamp will not get deleted
text:
### What happened?

```
$ cat cm.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: "2025-08-05T16:42:06Z"
  deletionTimestamp: 2025-08-05T16:42:06Z
  name: test
  namespace: kubevirt-vm

$ k apply --server-side -f cm.yaml
Warning: Detected changes to resource test which is currently being deleted.
configmap/test serverside-applied

$ k get cm test  -oyaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  creationTimestamp: "2025-08-05T16:45:59Z"
  deletionTimestamp: "2025-08-05T16:42:06Z"
  name: test
  namespace: kubevirt-vm
  resourceVersion: "257079249"
  uid: caa288c7-9b51-4365-a062-a4020d4bef93
```

Then deleting it causes it to go, so I am guessing this is rooted in insufficient event handling in the GC controller, since only SSA allows to create objects with a non-nil deletion timestamp:
```
$ k delete    -f cm.yaml
configmap "test" deleted

$ k get cm test  -oyaml
Error from server (NotFound): configmaps "test" not found
```

text: Scheduler's unschedulable_pods metric is not decremented when the pod is deleted from activeQ or backoffQ

title: Scheduler's unschedulable_pods metric is not decremented when the pod is deleted from activeQ or backoffQ
text:
### What happened?

When the queued pod with UnschedulablePlugins is removed from the scheduler entirely, the `unschedulable_pods` is not incremented. It's currently performed only for pods from `unschedulablePods` structure:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/03779bbd00da25c7fcd03711ddfe466a3322e1d7/pkg/scheduler/backend/queue/scheduling_queue.go#L1212-L1218

/sig scheduling

section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:

The `unschedulable_pods` to be decremented correctly for pods from activeQ or backoffQ.

title: Scheduler's unschedulable_pods metric is not decremented when the pod is deleted from activeQ or backoffQ
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create the pod that fails to schedule due to, e.g., resource constraints. Trigger its requeueing by freeing up the space, but remove it from the cluster completely before it's popped by the scheduling cycle.

title: Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
text:
Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser

While tracking https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126831, I found that implementing an optimized parser for this specific use case(which isn't at runtime) is not worthwhile. However, it would be great if there were a way to skip `YAMLObject` validation.

title: Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
text:
### Expected Improvement

With this change, #126831 can be closed, as YAMLObject validation is most time consuming task in this context.
Additionally, the total test duration will decreased by approximately 15 seconds, if that matters.

I ran a simple benchmark on my machine. If you need a more structured benchmark, I'd be happy to provide one.

/kind feature
/sig structured-merge-diff
/area code-generation
/assign

title: Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
text:
>While tracking https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/126831, I found that implementing an optimized parser for this specific use case(which isn't at runtime) is not worthwhile. However, it would be great if there were a way to skip `YAMLObject` validation.
>
>### Suggestion
>
>1. Add a `NewParserWithoutValidation` function to [structured-merge-diff/v6/typed/parser.go](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/ebc1ccc491c944fa0633f147698e0dc02675051d/vendor/sigs.k8s.io/structured-merge-diff/v6/typed/parser.go#L53)
>
>```go
>func NewParser(schema YAMLObject) (*Parser, error) {
>	// YAML schema validation logic that takes 200-300ms with open api schema
>	_, err := ssParser.Type("schema").FromYAML(schema)
>	if err != nil {
>		return nil, fmt.Errorf("unable to validate schema: %v", err)
>	}
>	return NewParserWithoutValidation(schema)
>}
>
>func NewParserWithoutValidation(schema YAMLObject) (*Parser, error) {    // <---- new function
>	return create(schema)
>}
>```
>
>2. Modify [code-generator/cmd/ap

title: Add a way to skip YAMLObject validation when create a Parser
text:
plyconfiguration-gen/generators/internal.go](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/c5b4b133ce3252ee19b7167eb69a99d88fdefda8/staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/applyconfiguration-gen/generators/internal.go#L87) to generate the following code:
>
>```go
>func Parser() *typed.Parser {
>	parserOnce.Do(func() {
>		var err error
>		parser, err = typed.NewParserWithoutValidation(schemaYAML)  // <---- changed
>		if err != nil {
>			panic(fmt.Sprintf("Failed to parse schema: %v", err))
>		}
>	})
>	return parser
>}
>```
>
>Validation can be skipped for `schemaYAML` since it's a hard-coded, valid YAML string that is [marshaled during codegen](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/c5b4b133ce3252ee19b7167eb69a99d88fdefda8/staging/src/k8s.io/code-generator/cmd/applyconfiguration-gen/generators/internal.go#L67).

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

some slack discussion: https://kubernetes.slack.com/archives/C0AHLJ0EAEL/p1775561714916009

Just add a few retries (with some backoff) at the pod group binding cycle's pod binding API calls. 
How many retries? -> That is a open question. 

Options in my mind:
- always the same max retries. 
- decide the max retry proportionally to the group size.
- retries workloads that meet some criteria, like gang and/or bigger than a certain size, etc.

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
### Why is this needed?

When it comes to WAS, binding API call failures due to temporary network issue or kube-apiserver instability are the expensive issue (especially for huge workloads) because
- We need to reschedule pods that failed at binding API call again with another WAS scheduling cycle. WAS is expensive.
- Gang could end up with a stuck issue if users don't have any rescheduling component (descheduler, Kueue's PodsReadyTimeout, etc)
  - Example: One-rack-sized gang job pods get scheduled to rack-A. But, more than half bindings have failed (they go back to the queue). Smaller jobs later came and take over the remaining empty capacity on rack-A. The first job never lands until smaller jobs complete at the same timing, which could be nearly impossible.
  - Note: if the pod group has some scheduled pods, the scheduler tries to bind all the other pods onto the same domain. i.e., in this example, all the remaining pods have to go to rack-A.

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
So, the reliability of binding API calls matters more than usual pod-by-pod scheduling. Of course, retries don't solve the issue completely, but it would reduce the chance for workloads to get into these troubles at least.

Adding a few retries (which might lengthen the binding cycle time) makes sense to me because:
- If some temporary network issue or kube-apiserver instability happen, even if we immediately push failed pods back to the queue and proceed to next schedulings of other pods, next bindings might fail with the same reason. "immediately pushing back" doesn't give any value for us.
- The pod group binding for big workloads takes time anyway. Adding some few retry API calls doesn't matter.

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
Alternatively or additionally, we can use NNN to preserve the previous decisions _to some extent_, but NNN is easy to get taken over. The bigger workload it is, the more likely it could hit the temporary binding failures, the more likely its NNN could be taken over by others (simply because it has tons of pods), therefore the more they need a reliable binding retry anyway for such failure mode.

/sig scheduling

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
/triage accepted
/cc @kubernetes/sig-scheduling-leads

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
+1 to this. Scheduler currently retries the API calls that patch pod status:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/6e753bd2b4793152b55ad9cefd3130169fb1a749/pkg/scheduler/util/utils.go#L138
We could do something similar for binding (why we haven't already?).

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
Good point.
Retrying binding operations similar to how pod status patching is handled
makes sense, especially given both rely on API calls that can transiently
fail. Aligning these behaviors could improve scheduler resilience.
Do you see any edge cases where retrying binding might introduce
inconsistencies or duplicate work?

On Wed, Apr 8, 2026, 9:35 AM Maciej Skoczeń ***@***.***>
wrote:

> *macsko* left a comment (kubernetes/kubernetes#138255)
> <https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/138255#issuecomment-4204917838>
>
> +1 to this. Scheduler currently retries the API calls that patch pod
> status:
>
> https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/6e753bd2b4793152b55ad9cefd3130169fb1a749/pkg/scheduler/util/utils.go#L138
> We could do something similar for binding (why we haven't already?).
>

> <https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/138255#issuecomment-4204917838>,
> or unsubscribe

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
ns/unsubscribe-auth/B7VTNGVSRQAKART3NAI6TLD4UYFMJAVCNFSM6AAAAACXPU3ZMOVHI2DSMVQWIX3LMV43OSLTON2WKQ3PNVWWK3TUHM2DEMBUHEYTOOBTHA>
> .
> You are receiving this because you are subscribed to this thread.Message
> ID: ***@***.***>

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
> (why we haven't already?).

I think, when we only had the pod-by-pod scheduling, we did not want to retry each failure individually and rather we wanted to just rely on backoffQ for overall such transient failure scenarios. (then why we had retries only for status patch is mystery though, anyways..)
But, here we need more robust reliability on binding (and possibly the API calls in general at the binding phase) since retrying the entire scheduling is now much more expensive than pod-by-pod era.

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text: Hey, I'm new to k8s, can I work on this issue?

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
> we did not want to retry each failure individually and rather we wanted to just rely on backoffQ for overall such transient failure scenarios.

This is problematic in case there was a PreBind for the pod (#135771). In this case we would want to do the same kind of retry mechanism as you propose for WAS.

Maybe such retry mechanism could be added to bind regardless of PreBind or whether it's WAS?

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text:
> Maybe such retry mechanism could be added to bind regardless of PreBind or whether it's WAS?

Yes. Given everything will be group scheduling, they will be the same after all. We should just retry any API calls within binding cycles.

title: WAS: Retry binding API calls on pod group binding cycle
text: I talked to @macsko and @dom4ha and we will add retrying for every binding call with the same parameters as we have for patch pod status

title: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.
text: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.

title: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.
text:
We have a mechanical hard drive with relatively low disk I/O. A pod has mounted a PVC, which contains approximately 700,000 files. When the pod is rebuilt, after the CSI volume is normally mounted, it checks the directory permissions of the volume. Since the fsGroupChangePolicy is configured as OnRootMismatch and the mount directory permissions are set to 750, our code's requiresPermissionChange method checks if the directory permissions are 770. This results in permission changes being made every time the volume is mounted. If there are a large number of files and the disk is slow, the mounting process can take a very long time.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/948afe5ca072329a73c8e79ed5938717a5cb3d21/pkg/volume/volume_linux.go#L131-L155
Additionally, there is another issue: the permissions of the created mount directory are set to 750. Why is there a subsequent check to determine if the permissions are 770?
The code for creating a directory is as follows: 
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernet

title: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.
text: es/blob/948afe5ca072329a73c8e79ed5938717a5cb3d21/pkg/volume/csi/csi_mounter.go#L212-L216

title: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.
text:
There must be something odd in the CSI driver. Indeed, the mount directory is created with `0750` (and it might be an oversight). But the CSI driver is supposed to **mount** something there. I.e. after the mount, there will be root directory of the volume. Its permissions are unrelated to the directory that kubelet created.

What CSI driver do you use?

title: The kubelet process always recursively modifies the volume file permissions.
text:
Since https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/113225 was merged, the csi driver should apply the fsGroup to the volume when the NodePublishVolume rpc is called. kubelet only take over this work when the csi driver does not support NodeServiceCapability_RPC_VOLUME_MOUNT_GROUP.

And the mountpoint is created by the csi driver, not kubelet.

title: KEP-5304: DecodeMetadataFromStream silently skips malformed metadata for registered API versions
text: KEP-5304: DecodeMetadataFromStream silently skips malformed metadata for registered API versions

title: KEP-5304: DecodeMetadataFromStream silently skips malformed metadata for registered API versions
text:
## What happened?

devicemetadata.DecodeMetadataFromStream in staging/src/k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation/devicemetadata/decode.go treats all per-entry decode failures the same way: it records them in an internal skippedErrors slice and continues to the next stream entry. The only signal bubbled out is a single aggregated error, and only when no entry in the stream decoded successfully.

The practical consequence: if a DRA driver writes a metadata stream whose registered entry (e.g. metadata.resource.k8s.io/v1alpha1) is malformed, and the stream happens to contain any decodable entry after it, the malformed entry is silently swallowed. The caller, if a producer-side bug has occurred.

title: KEP-5304: DecodeMetadataFromStream silently skips malformed metadata for registered API versions
text:
## What you expected to happen

The decoder should distinguish between two failure modes:

1. Unknown apiVersion (producer is ahead of consumer): silent skip is correct — this is the forward-compatibility contract the JSON-stream design exists to support.
2. Registered apiVersion but decode/convert fails (malformed object, missing kind, missing apiVersion, wrong field shape, etc.): this is a producer bug. It should be surfaced to the caller even when decoding succeeds via a later stream entry, so the operator can diagnose and fix the driver.

As a tertiary concern, apiVersion-less entries are currently lumped into the silent-skip bucket, which is also wrong: they indicate malformed metadata, not forward compatibility.

title: KEP-5304: DecodeMetadataFromStream silently skips malformed metadata for registered API versions
text:
## How to reproduce

Will provide a unit test that re-creates this error easily.

More discussion here: https://github.com/kubevirt/kubevirt/pull/17028#discussion_r3106470305

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
text: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
section: ### What would you like to be added?

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
text:
### Why is this needed?

During benchmarking, we found that deserialization in client-go appears to be a bottleneck when handling Watch requests. I built a stress test, and the results show that JSON encoding is significantly less efficient than PB or CBOR. As object size increases, JSON deserialization throughput drops rapidly.

And before formally submitting a PR or KEP, I would like to first gather feedback from the community.

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
text:
The test uses Pod objects. Although Pods are usually encoded with PB in most cases, I believe the result is still meaningful for operations on CR objects. On the other hand, I understand that newer versions have added CBOR encoding to improve performance. However, because client-go remains compatible with older versions of the apiserver, and there are many deployments in the wild where a newer client-go is used with a slightly older control plane, I believe optimizing JSON deserialization performance in client-go is still valuable.

Currently, the deserialization path inside client-go is entirely serial and single-core. Inspired by the ConcurrentWatchObjectDecode feature gate in the apiserver, I think a similar mechanism could also be applied to client-go.

In terms of implementation, after further profiling, I found that the JSON path attempts deserialization three times:

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
text:
1. The JSON framer splits JSON objects from the chunked body.
2. The outer Event object is deserialized.
3. The actual object inside the Event is deserialized.

Among these, the first step cannot be parallelized, but the other two can. I verified the idea of parallelizing steps 2 and 3 locally, but also found that step 1 then became the bottleneck:

title: Concurrent decoding objects for watching requests in client-go
text: I wonder whether we could modify the JSON framer so that it does not validate the full JSON syntax, and instead uses a more relaxed approach for framing. To test this, I implemented a state machine that only matches closing braces/brackets to split the stream, and on my machine it reached around 500-900MB/s. In addition, after reading the code further, I found that Watch responses have historically never used pretty-printed output. Therefore, if we relax the semantics, we could actually split by newline. Under this assumption, throughput on my machine can be further improved to more than 5GB/s, which would completely remove the first deserialization bottleneck:

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
### What happened?

When we tried to upgrade to K8s 1.33, with the environment varaible `KUBE_WATCHCACHE_CONSISTENCY_CHECKER` enabled, the panic "Cache inconsistency check failed" happened and all api servers kept terminating and restarting.

We had two clusters which experienced this issue. One recovered within a few hours, but second one could not recover for 7 days until we disbaled the environment variable `KUBE_WATCHCACHE_CONSISTENCY_CHECKER`.

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
One possible root cause which we have not verified:

Although Etcd guarantees all updates/events for a single transaction come in a single watch response, the watch cache, as a Etcd watch client, is processing the watch events one by one([code](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.33/staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache/reflector.go#L874)), not observing Etcd’s transactional semantics, leaving it possible that Etcd and watch cache buffer have different set of objects even for a same resource version, especially in busy update situation.

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
Would like to clarify that the above theory is unlikely to be the root cause. Since the Kubernetes etcd interface only modifies a single key per transaction, multi-key transactions should only be generated by etcd lease expiry. We only use etcd leases for events, and for this reason, watch cache is disabled by default for events as described in #131897 and #110210.

So the above theory is only feasible if there's a separate bug causing etcd to send multiple watch events with the same revision.

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
+1 to what @rowantran said.

I'm surprised that you observed issue in 2 clusters including consistent failures. This check has been running K8s in e2e tests since 1.33 and was enabled by default in 1.34 (without panic). I didn't observe any failures, also counting in GKE.

I would need to know more about you setup to understand what's unique in your environment that is triggering it. Things like etcd version specifically. There were some of issues with etcd watch that were addressed https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/tree/main/tests/robustness#robustness-track-record

Note: `KUBE_WATCHCACHE_CONSISTENCY_CHECKER` environment variable for to enable strict validation in testing, not expected to be enabled in production. In 1.34 we enabled the feature by default to expose metric `apiserver_storage_consistency_checks_total` and not panic.

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
Sharing some finding: when the object key in etcd is corrupted but the value is intact, we will see the consistency checker complain. This is because consistency checker is checking etcd values.
A concrete example: a lease keyed by X/Y where X is namespace and Y is name was corrupted to be keyed as X/Z. The controller that owns lease X/Y will recreate a new lease keyed X/Y. When listing from etcd, you will get 2 objects: 1) key: X/Y, value: X/Y 2) key: X/Z, value: X/Y. When you list from watch cache, you will only see 1 object: 1) key: X/Y, value: X/Y

title: apiserver: Watch cache consistency check failure constantly panics apiserver instances
text:
Thanks for reopening! I’ll keep an eye on this and help move it forward
where I can

wrote:

>
> Sharing some finding: when the object key in etcd is corrupted but the
> value is intact, we will see the consistency checker complain. This is
> because consistency checker is checking etcd values.
> A concrete example: a lease keyed by X/Y where X is namespace and Y is
> name was corrupted to be keyed as X/Z. The controller that owns lease X/Y
> will recreate a new lease keyed X/Y. When listing from etcd, you will get 2
> objects: 1) key: X/Y, value: X/Y 2) key: X/Z, value: X/Y. When you list
> from watch cache, you will only see 1 object: 1) key: X/Y, value: X/Y
>

title: Excessive events generated for Image Volumes (every image, once per minute)
text:
The actual problem this is causing me is a CI failure due to excessive events, but I think this is objectively too many events.

I'm not familiar with this code, but it seems [`getImageVolumes`](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b75d1f6dca60658a5b293e4cedb90cdb4f0b3293/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L1866-L1867), which is called from SyncPod re-uses [`EnsureImageExists`](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b75d1f6dca60658a5b293e4cedb90cdb4f0b3293/pkg/kubelet/images/image_manager.go#L164-L165), which is also called by `startContainer`. `EnsureImageExists` logs unconditionally, which is where the noise comes from. I suspect this unconditional logging is appropriate in `startContainer`, but perhaps not in `getImageVolumes` when called from the reconcile loop?

title: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission
text: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission

title: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission
text: ### NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission

title: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission
text:
This issue was reported in the [Kubernetes 1.24 Security Audit Report](https://github.com/kubernetes/sig-security/blob/main/sig-security-external-audit/security-audit-2021-2022/findings/Kubernetes%20v1.24%20Final%20Report.pdf)

**Impact**
A user with permissions on the `nodes/proxy` subresource in a cluster has full permissions
against the kubelet API on any node by proxying requests through the API server, and can
execute commands in any pod. This may represent privileges beyond those expected by
the cluster administrator, and this situation is not explained in the Kubernetes
documentation.

**Description**
The Kubernetes API server proxy is a feature which allows users to proxy requests through
the API server to workloads or nodes. When a proxy request is made to the kubelet service
on a node, the API server’s own client certificate (which in a typical cluster is a user in the
`system:masters` group) is used to authenticate the request. This effectively allows a user with `get` permissions agai

title: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission
text:
nst the `nodes/proxy` subresource to make `GET` requests to the kubelet
API as `system:masters` (and equivalent for `create`, `patch`, etc.). It is not clear from the
Kubernetes documentation that giving a user permission to make proxied requests to nodes is effectively permitting them to run arbitrary commands against any pod (as can be achieved using the Kubelet API’s `/exec` endpoint).

**Recommendation**
Ensure that the Kubernetes documentation makes it clear that permissions on the `nodes/
proxy` subresource are powerful, overriding other permissions against nodes (as shown
above) and permitting commands to be run in pods.

**Component**
kubelet


**Anything else we need to know?**
See umbrella issue [#118980](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/118980) for current status of all issues created from these findings.
The vendor gave this issue an ID of NCC-E003660-WHE and it was finding 18 of the report under the kube-apiserver section.
The vendor considers this issue Medium Risk

title: NCC-E003660-WV3: Privilege Escalation via `nodes/proxy` Permission
text:
, High Impact, Low Exploitability
To view the original finding, begin on page 49 of the [Kubernetes 1.24 Security Audit Report](https://github.com/kubernetes/sig-security/blob/main/sig-security-external-audit/security-audit-2021-2022/findings/Kubernetes%20v1.24%20Final%20Report.pdf)

**Test Environment**
Kubernetes 1.24.3

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
### What happened?

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/apis/core/types.go#L2110-L2113
```
	RecursiveReadOnly *RecursiveReadOnlyMode
	// Required. If the path is not an absolute path (e.g. some/path) it
	// will be prepended with the appropriate root prefix for the operating
	// system.  On Linux this is '/', on Windows this is 'C:\'.
	MountPath string
```
but
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/api/core/v1/types.go#L2283
```
	// Path within the container at which the volume should be mounted.  Must
	// not contain ':'.
	MountPath string `json:"mountPath" protobuf:"bytes,3,opt,name=mountPath"`
```
some people use mountpath:/etc/localtime:ro (k8s should use readonly:true)  will not work on containerd but work on docker.

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
![aaaaa](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c0d854b2-4f72-476d-a6b9-7361f10aa0c9)
some user use wrong yaml but works on docker. (k8s with docker )
when use it with containerd containerd will create a file "localtime:ro" (k8s with containerd)
So I think we can prevent to use ":ro" or ":rw"

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text: We don't support colons in the path, or we shouldn't, because runtimes like docker do the wrong thing.  Have those all been fixed?  I do not think special-casing ":ro" makes a lot sense.  Runtimes should fix support for colons, or not support them at all.

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
cc @brendandburns @pires 
Can you have a look? because you are main reviewers of https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/31707

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text: Is the issue here about Windows?  I don't have any first hand background here - if we needed to special case colons on Windows, to manage drive letters, I could maybe understand that. But I thought that was a solved problem already?

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
I think this issue is related to the windows because the constraint is removed when kubeneretes support windows nodes. And there're a lot of e2e tests and unit tests which uses `c:/` as mount path's prefix. 

`:` is valid character in windows or linux path. so it's acceptable to use it in kubernetes. We should treat it as a part of path. it doesn't represent any special meaning (i.e. mount options). It is different from docker. 

The CRI standard said that the mount path is the [path of the mount within the container](https://github.com/carlory/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/cri-api/pkg/apis/runtime/v1/api.proto#L222). So the issue should be fixed in the CRI implementation, not in the kubernetes.

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
> Runtimes should fix support for colons, or not support them at all.

+1. It should depend on the implementation of container runtime. I don't have more knowledge about docker cri since docker shim was removed from in-tree.

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
I don't mean to flip on this one, but it's a very long-standing issue.

My feeling is that we should do what the most commonly used runtimes support .  It seems like docker has a way to mount on paths with colons via the API, now.  Presumably that means containers, too.  Does CRI-O? 

On Windows, colon has two meanings - one is drive letters, which is obvious, but the other is file streams, which is NOT what we want.  A quick Google suggests that colons are NOT allowed in directory names on Windows 

If someone wants to champion this, we will need details about how each runtime supports colons as part of the analysis.

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
Test results: we can see the same result as containerd. ':' is a part of the file name. @thockin 

```console
(base) ➜  ~ kubectl get no -oyaml | grep containerRuntimeVersion
      containerRuntimeVersion: cri-o://1.31.0
      containerRuntimeVersion: cri-o://1.31.0
(base) ➜  ~ kubectl exec -it test-pod -- ls -l /zone/etc/
total 4
-rw-r--r--    1 root     root           114 Feb  3  2024 localtime:ro
(base) ➜  ~ cat pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: test-pod
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: test-volume
    hostPath:
      path: /etc/localtime
  containers:
  - name: test-pod
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - mountPath: /zone/etc/localtime:ro
      name: test-volume
```

title: can MountPath contain  ":" ?
text:
How about CRI-O?

If we want to change how this works, someone has to write a KEP to explore the various compatibility questions and productionability issues. For example, API validation is done very early before we necessarily know what OS is going to be used.  Is there enough information in a pod spec to ensure that colons would be allowed on Linux and not allowed on Windows? Or is it okay for it to just fail on Windows? I'm sure there are many questions. That's what AKEP is for

text:
> How about CRI-O?  

the above example is based on  CRI-O

/priority backlog

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
text: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
text:
### What happened?

The kubelet's local-storage eviction logic that enforces per-container `resources.limits.ephemeral-storage` only iterates `pod.Spec.Containers`. Restartable init containers (`initContainers[].restartPolicy: Always`, i.e. native sidecars) are never compared against their declared per-container limit, so a sidecar can exceed its `limits.ephemeral-storage` indefinitely without triggering pod eviction.

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
text: Note: the pod-level check (`podEphemeralStorageLimitEviction`) is not a reliable backstop, since it will only fire when every regular container also sets an ephemeral-storage limit (otherwise `PodLimits()` returns no ephemeral-storage entry and the function returns early). Also, LimitRange *does* default/enforce ephemeral-storage limits onto init containers at admission (`plugin/pkg/admission/limitranger/admission.go`), so an operator who configures a LimitRange reasonably expects the kubelet to honor the limit it stamped on the sidecar.

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

A restartable init container that exceeds its `resources.limits.ephemeral-storage` should cause the pod to be evicted with reason `Evicted` / message referencing ephemeral-storage, identical to the behavior for regular containers.

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sidecar-ephemeral-repro
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: sidecar
    image: busybox:1.36
    restartPolicy: Always
    command: ["sh", "-c", "dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/fill bs=1M count=500; sleep 1d"]
    resources:
      limits:
        ephemeral-storage: 10Mi
  containers:
  - name: main
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["sleep", "1d"]
    # deliberately no ephemeral-storage limit, to also disable the pod-level check
```
Apply, wait >30s. Observe: pod stays `Running`; `kubectl get pod sidecar-ephemeral-repro -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'` never transitions to `Failed`. Replacing the sidecar with an identical regular container under `spec.containers` causes eviction within one monitoring interval.

title: kubelet: per-container ephemeral-storage limit not enforced for restartable init containers (sidecars)
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

- Node-level disk-pressure eviction and taints are unaffected, so this is a per-container limit-semantics bug, not a node-DoS vector. Filing publicly for that reason; happy to re-route via the security process if SIG Node disagrees.
- Proposed fix is small: also iterate `pod.Spec.InitContainers` when building `thresholdsMap`, optionally restricted to `podutil.IsRestartableInitContainer(&c)` so run-to-completion init containers are skipped. Willing to send a PR + unit test in `pkg/kubelet/eviction/eviction_manager_test.go`.

title: Snapshot PodGroup object before scheduling cycle
text: Snapshot PodGroup object before scheduling cycle

title: Snapshot PodGroup object before scheduling cycle
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Add PodGroup object into the snapshot, and update snapshot before scheduling cycle with such object.

title: Snapshot PodGroup object before scheduling cycle
text:
### Why is this needed?

When PodGroup object becomes mutable, snapshotting PodGroup will become necessary to avoid some disruptions.

But a sufficient reason to snapshot it now, is to detect the deletion of a PodGroup during scheduling. We need to have a safety measure against that delete action and immediately abort podgroup scheduling cycle.

This was discussed in context of this Pull Request: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137073#discussion_r2846567715
This requirement is blocked on implementation of PodGroup in V1Alpha2 which currently resides in this Pull Request: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136976

text: Limitation of more than 64 backend pods for a Single Service on Windows nodes needs to be removed from documentation

title: Limitation of more than 64 backend pods for a Single Service on Windows nodes needs to be removed from documentation
text:
### What happened?

https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/windows-networking/#limitations states the following as one of the things _not_ supported:
> More than 64 backend pods (or unique destination addresses) for a single Service

The original trigger for this documentation is https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/98680 apparently, which led to https://github.com/microsoft/Windows-Containers/issues/93. Comments in the latter issue say that the issue has been fixed with the limit increased to 1024. By now (3 years later), I believe the limitation has been fixed in Windows Server in both 2019 and 2022.

title: [Scheduler] Add an extension after PostFilter that will always execute
text: [Scheduler] Add an extension after PostFilter that will always execute

title: [Scheduler] Add an extension after PostFilter that will always execute
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

I am a plugin developer, the plugin will have different actions according to the preemption result. For example, mark the podGorup (API in schedule-plugins) as failed and reject all the pods when failed to preempt, or I will mark the pods as schedulable (I will limit the number of times to schedule any pods in PodGroup for the performance).
But when any of the plugin return code other than fwk.Unschedulable, scheduler will skip all plugins after that. This makes difficult to implement the PostFilter:
- If we run coscheduling before preemption, we can not know the preempt result.
- If we run coscheduling after preemption and preempt return error (for example the pod is deleted when in schedulingCycle), we are not able to reject the podGroup because the scheduling stop the iteration for the PostFilter.

title: [Scheduler] Add an extension after PostFilter that will always execute
text:
### Why is this needed?

Mentioned above.

title: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition
text: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition

title: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition
text:
### What happened?

We observed that in cluster with phase=Failed pods present, the update of the deployment using Recreate strategy was stalling for ~10 minutes (ProgressDeadlineSeconds period).

After a log analysis and code, I think this is caused by the fact that [deletePod handler](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/74e84a90c725047b1328ff3d589fedb1cb7a120e/pkg/controller/deployment/deployment_controller.go#L385-L391) (the handler attached to pod informer), for Recreate case is enqueuing the deployment only if the number of pods equals zero. OTOH if the loop triggers, the different condition is checked: [oldPodsRunning](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/74e84a90c725047b1328ff3d589fedb1cb7a120e/pkg/controller/deployment/recreate.go#L48-L51) ignores the terminal state pods such as Failed/Succeeded.

In effect, we may not enqueue deployment at the time when oldPodsRunning becomes true in a case when e.g. Succeeded pod is present.

title: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

That the deployment is enqueued at the time when oldPodsRunning becomes true -- the last pod is deleted.

title: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

* Create a deployment with a pod in Failed state (we used a deployment with image that OOMs quite often)
* Try to update deployment few times
* You will observe that the updates starts to be delayed by ~10m at some point.

title: Deployment controller: Inconsistency of deletePod pod update handler and oldPodsRunning condition
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

A proposed fix is to change the deletePod to exclude the Final/Succeed state or even better share the logic with the oldPodsRunning condition (maybe even call it from there).

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Add support for seamless takeover by a new device-plugin for a resource without deregistering the resource.

In other words, a new device-plugin can be started and registering the resource before the old device-plugin is stopped. In this case, devicemanager should not change the values of allocatable/total count for this resource.

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text:
### Why is this needed?

Currently, devicemanager only works with one device-plugin instance for every resource. In other words, if two device-plugin are running, devicemanager may have unexpected behaviors. 

For example, when two device-plugin are running, they are both registered for the same resource. However, if one of them stops, the resource is marked as deregistered and resource counts reset to 0.

We need this support to have seamless deployment of device-plugin. After this is supported, new device-plugin can be started while the old device-plugin is also running, and devicemanager should notice the new device-plugin, as it registers more recently, to be the new takeover and gracefully deregister/disconnect the old device-plugin. With this, there will be no downtime for resources in device-plugin updates.

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text:
This seems interesting. What's the usecase for this feature? Which user flow benefit from this?

In addition: how can we safely distinguish a takeover, so we avoid to report stale state from the kubelet?

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text:
The major use case will be updating device-plugin Pods seamlessly. The updating device-plugin pods would now stop the old pod and then start the new pod, the time period between them is the downtime for the resources as their allocatable/total counts would be reset to 0.

Whoever comes later is the takeover, and devicemanager should disconnect the previous device-plugin after new registration of same resource.

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text:
> The major use case will be updating device-plugin Pods seamlessly. The updating device-plugin pods would now stop the old pod and then start the new pod, the time period between them is the downtime for the resources as their allocatable/total counts would be reset to 0.
> 
> Whoever comes later is the takeover, and devicemanager should disconnect the previous device-plugin after new registration of same resource.

Thanks, this help. Does the current upgrade flow cause some harm to the users? do we have some issues reported about that?

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text: It seems that Meza is great and very ideal. I am working to benefit from his experience and integrate the new basic concepts and address the previous versions and update the definitions.

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text: The impact may be not that visible if the cluster has only a handful of nodes with resources. I didn't search for existing issues but my own observation on a large cluster of thousands of GPUs was that whenever there is an update for the device plugin pod for the cluster, a visible amount of nodes report 0 GPU counts for a short period of time.

title: Support takeover for devicemanager/device-plugin
text:
> The impact may be not that visible if the cluster has only a handful of nodes with resources. I didn't search for existing issues but my own observation on a large cluster of thousands of GPUs was that whenever there is an update for the device plugin pod for the cluster, a visible amount of nodes report 0 GPU counts for a short period of time.

yes, the counts dropping to 0 is how it is meant to work with the current code. I'm trying to understand if the counter reset to 0 has some negative effect on workload.
I can think the following: some nodes won't be used by the scheduler from a short period of time till the node publishes the updated state again. Are there more issues or this is it?

I'm asking because the takeover doesn't look trivial to implement in the current architecture, so changes must be evaluated carefully in terms of cost/benefit ration

That's it. But it causes noises in monitoring as it seems like the devices are not available for a while. Seamless update would be ideal.

text:
Please correct me if I'm wrong, according to https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/workload-resources/daemon-set-v1/#DaemonSetSpec

When updateStrategy.rollingUpdate.maxSurge is specified:

> Example: when this is set to 30%, at most 30% of the total number of nodes that should be running the daemon pod (i.e. status.desiredNumberScheduled) can have their a new pod created before the old pod is marked as deleted. The update starts by launching new pods on 30% of nodes. Once an updated pod is available (Ready for at least minReadySeconds) the old DaemonSet pod on that node is marked deleted.

My understanding on this is for those nodes there can be 2 device-plugin pods running at the same time for the period of update.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text:
### What happened?

The issue only occurred after upgrading Windows Server OS 2019 from Build `17763.8027` to Build `17763.8146`. The Windows nodes depends on Windows networking stack, corresponding `kube-proxy` without CNI.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text:
On Windows nodes, when a LoadBalancer or NodePort service has its backing endpoints scaled to 0, `kube-proxy` fails to release the underlying Host Networking Service (HNS) port lock. When endpoints are scaled back up, `kube-proxy` attempts to recreate the policy, hits a collision with the orphaned lock, and enters a crash loop, throwing the following Windows HNS error:
```
proxier.go:1604] "Nodeport policy creation failed" err="hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists. (0x803b0013) {\"Success\":false,\"Error\":\"The sp
ecified port already exists. \",\"ErrorCode\":2151350291}" nodeport=30364 sourceVip="" proxierIPFamily="IPv4" nodeIP="10.0.0.4"
```
This is exposed by a recent OS-level change. On older Windows Server builds (e.g., `17763.8027`), the OS silently forgave the leak and allowed overwriting the inactive policy. On newer strict builds (e.g., `17763.8146`), HNS strictly rejects the overwrite, making explicit cleanup a hard functional requirement.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text: Also seeing [oad Balancer already exists.](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/proxy/winkernel/proxier.go#L1407) while it is clearly commented as **"//This should not happen"**.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

`kube-proxy` should successfully delete the old Windows HNS LoadBalancer policy when endpoints drop to 0, freeing the OS port lock, and cleanly recreate the policy when new endpoints are available.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Provision a Kubernetes cluster with a Windows worker node running OS Build `17763.8146` or newer.
2. Deploy a `LoadBalancer` service and a backing deployment (replicas: 1).
3. Scale the deployment to 0: `kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=0`.
4. Scale the deployment back to 1: `kubectl scale deployment <name> --replicas=1`.
5. Observe the kube-proxy logs on the Windows node. It will fail with:
`err="hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists. (0x803b0013)`.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

Restarting the `kube-proxy` pod on the affected node temporarily clears the issue. During its startup synchronization, `kube-proxy` correctly identifies the orphaned HNS LoadBalancers and garbage-collects them. However, the standard runtime sync loop continues to leak them.

This likely exposed a bug in the cleanup logic within `syncProxyRules`, so the NodePort LB remains active in Windows HNS with a lock on the port. When a new Endpoint ID spins up, the hash changes so a new NodePort policy is created, which leads to `0x803b0013`.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text:
@qiyueyao ,

I’ve been working on bringing up an AKS cluster with Windows Server 2019 to try out the steps you shared, but then I got to know that Windows Server 2019 is no longer supported starting from Kubernetes version 1.33 onward.

Below is the error message encountered:

```(Windows2019NotSupportedWithOrchestratorVersion) Cannot create Windows2019 node pools with the current Kubernetes version "1.34.3" because Windows2019 was deprecated after Kubernetes version "1.33.0". Please create Windows2022 node pools instead.```

Given this, any changes made to KubeProxy to address the issue would not be backportable beyond version 1.34, as version 1.33 is already end-of-life. Additionally, if fixes were to depend on Windows Server 2019, customers would still need to move to Kubernetes 1.34 or later for future support, which in turn requires upgrading to Windows Server 2022 or newer.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text:
Considering these constraints, this path appears to be a dead end, as we currently do not have a viable option to implement a supported fix in this scenario.

As there is no supported path for fixes on either Windows Server 2019 or AKS versions beyond 1.33, we kindly recommend that the customer consider upgrading to Windows Server 2022 or a later version.

Please let me know if you’d like to discuss this further.

title: [sig-windows] kube-proxy crash loop: hcnCreateLoadBalancer failed in Win32: The specified port already exists (0x803b0013)
text: @princepereira Thank you for confirming the support matrix and taking the time to test this! I completely agree given that Windows Server 2019 is deprecated in Kubernetes 1.33+, trying to pursue a workaround in `kube-proxy` for this specific OS version is a dead end. I am closing this issue, thank you again for your guidance!

DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
text: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
text:
### What happened?

I am using DRA with a CEL selector to filter devices based on driver attributes. When writing an expression that accesses a nested field within `device.attributes`, the scheduler encounters a CEL runtime error if the specific driver key does not exist on the device, rather than evaluating the expression to false.

The following expression fails during the DynamicResources filter plugin execution:

```
cel:
  expression: 'device.attributes["gpu.nvidia.com"].sharingStrategy == "runai-fractions"'
```

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

I expected the expression to evaluate to false for devices that do not possess the gpu.nvidia.com attribute, allowing the scheduler to simply skip those devices.

Instead, the scheduler treats this as a hard runtime error, preventing the claim from being allocated even if valid devices exist, or logging significant errors during the filter phase.

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

```
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: gpu.nvidia.com
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel: 
      expression: "device.driver == 'gpu.nvidia.com'"
---
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceSlice
metadata:
  name: kind-control-plane-gpu-devices-slice
spec:
  nodeName: kind-control-plane
  driver: gpu.nvidia.com

pool:
    generation: 1
    resourceSliceCount: 1
    name: kind-control-plane-gpu-pool

devices:
  - name: gpu-0
    bindsToNode: true

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
- name: gpu-1
    attributes:
      sharingStrategy:
        string: runai-fractions
    bindsToNode: true
---
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1
kind: ResourceClaim
metadata:
  name: kind-control-plane-gpu-runai-fractionable-claim-1
spec:
  devices:
    requests:
    - name: gpu-runai-fractionable
      exactly:
        deviceClassName: gpu.nvidia.com
        capacity:
          requests:
            memory: 10Gi
        selectors:
        - cel:
            expression: 'device.attributes["gpu.nvidia.com"].sharingStrategy == "runai-fractions"'
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: fraction-pod-1
spec:
  resourceClaims:
  - name: fraction-claim
    resourceClaimName: kind-control-plane-gpu-runai-fractionable-claim-1
  containers:
  - name: fraction-container
    resources:
      claims:
      - name: fraction-claim
    image: ubuntu:latest
    command: [ "/bin/bash", "-c", "--" ]
    args: [sleep Infinity"]
```

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
```
> kubectl describe pod fraction-pod-1
...
Events:
  Type     Reason            Age              From               Message
  ----     ------            ----             ----               -------
  Warning  FailedScheduling  3s               default-scheduler  0/1 nodes are available: 1 cannot allocate all claims. still not schedulable, preemption: 0/1 nodes are available: 1 Preemption is not helpful for scheduling.
  Warning  FailedScheduling  1s (x2 over 3s)  default-scheduler  running "DynamicResources" filter plugin: claim default/kind-control-plane-gpu-runai-fractionable-claim-1: selector #0: CEL runtime error: no such key: sharingStrategy
```

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
text:
This works as designed and described:

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/04d87a4b6e72336ee9afb1e5b477223c96a8fcbb/staging/src/k8s.io/api/resource/v1/types.go#L1200-L1206

Tolerating this as you expected could have the effect that devices are not allocated as expected, so we wanted users to be aware that they have to be more precise in their selector.

What we can do is extend the documentation with an example:

device.attributes["gpu.nvidia.com"].?sharingStrategy.orValue("") == "runai-fractions"'

This falls back to the empty string if `sharingStrategy` is not defined.

title: DRA: Scheduler CEL evaluation fails with "no such key" when accessing nested attributes without existence check
text:
Hi @pohly, I saw you're planning to extend the documentation with CEL expression examples for this issue. I'd be happy to help with this!

I've been working on DRA-related code recently (device-manager improvements) and would like to contribute to better documentation around CEL selector patterns. Specifically I could help with:

1. **Adding examples to DRA documentation** - Show common patterns like `orValue()`, `has()`, and `exists()` for handling optional attributes

2. **Improving error messages** - When CEL throws "no such key" errors, we could add hints suggesting the use of `orValue()` or similar methods

3. **Adding test coverage** - Document and test proper CEL expression patterns to prevent future confusion

Would you welcome collaboration on this? If so, I can start with a documentation PR covering the CEL selector patterns. Let me know what would be most helpful!

text: Kubelet starts pods before CNI is configured

title: Kubelet starts pods before CNI is configured
text:
### What happened?

First time Setup for a standalone k8s was fine and applied flannel for cni.

The issue was kubelet starting the pods before my cni is able to fully initialized with /run/flannel/subnet.env on every single restart. Hence i will stumble upon this error 

"Warning  FailedCreatePodSandBox  6m36s                 kubelet            Failed to create pod sandbox: rpc error: code = Unknown desc = failed to setup network for sandbox "8893b3a6a2ad1b3496a2dc918fd6be8c1a8ef1e72eab286fccfe1b1d7a061dfd": plugin type="flannel" failed (add): failed to load flannel 'subnet.env' file: open /run/flannel/subnet.env: no such file or directory. Check the flannel pod log for this node."

Then the whole thing will restart again, by that time my cni has already fully initialized and back to normal.

text:
### What did you expect to happen?

i expect that if i somehow delay the kubelet startup time and wait for the cni to fully initialize, but it just keep the kubelet on the restart loop forever.

title: Deployment stuck indefinitely when scaling during rolling update with maxSurge=0 due to stale annotations
text: Deployment stuck indefinitely when scaling during rolling update with maxSurge=0 due to stale annotations

title: Deployment stuck indefinitely when scaling during rolling update with maxSurge=0 due to stale annotations
text:
### What happened?

When a Deployment is scaled down during an in-progress rolling update (with maxSurge=0),
  the deployment controller gets stuck in an infinite loop and the rolling update never completes.

The issue occurs because:
  1. During a rolling update, ReplicaSet annotations (deployment.kubernetes.io/desired-replicas)
     are set to the initial deployment size
  2. If the deployment is scaled down while the update is in progress, the replica count changes
     but the annotations remain stale
  3. isScalingEvent() detects the annotation mismatch and returns true, causing the controller
     to call sync() instead of rolloutRolling()
  4. scale() checks if replica counts match and returns early without updating annotations
     (lines 303-304 in pkg/controller/deployment/sync.go)
  5. scaleReplicaSetAndRecordEvent() also has an early return when replicas match (lines 400-401)
  6. The annotations are never updated, creating an infinite loop

title: Deployment stuck indefinitely when scaling during rolling update with maxSurge=0 due to stale annotations
text:
This prevents the rolling update from proceeding. Pods remain on the old version indefinitely, and there are no error messages.

**Impact:** Applications cannot be updated, security patches cannot be deployed, and the
  deployment appears healthy while being completely stuck.

title: Deployment stuck indefinitely when scaling during rolling update with maxSurge=0 due to stale annotations
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

The deployment controller should update ReplicaSet annotations even when replica counts
  already match the desired state. After scaling during a rolling update, the controller should:

  1. Detect the annotation mismatch
  2. Update the stale annotations to match the current deployment.spec.replicas
  3. Continue with the rolling update by calling rolloutRolling()
  4. Create a new ReplicaSet with the updated template
  5. Complete the rolling update successfully

  The annotations should be kept in sync with deployment.spec.replicas regardless of whether
  the replica count changed, to ensure isScalingEvent() correctly identifies true scaling
  events versus stale annotation states.

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
### What happened?

kube-proxy iptables mode sync latency is 5-6x slower on aws-k8s-1.34 nodes compared to aws-k8s-1.32/1.33 nodes.

| Node Variant | iptables version             | Rule Sync Latency | iptables-save time |
  |--------------|------------------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
  | aws-k8s-1.32 | iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)    | 32-52ms           | 21ms               |
  | aws-k8s-1.33 | iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)    | 24-34ms           | -                  |
  | aws-k8s-1.34 | iptables v1.8.11 (nf_tables) | 172-248ms         | 61ms               |

Test environment: Same EKS cluster , same kube-proxy version (v1.34.3-eksbuild.2), same workload (877 services, 2057 endpoints).

Root cause: aws-k8s-1.34 switched from legacy iptables to iptables-nft (nftables kernel backend with iptables compatibility layer). Every iptables operation is now translated to nftables, adding significant overhead.

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
Evidence from the nodes:
  # aws-k8s-1.32 (fast)
  $ iptables --version
  iptables v1.8.11 (legacy)

$ ls -la /usr/sbin/iptables*
  # Points to legacy iptables binaries

# aws-k8s-1.34 (slow)
  $ iptables --version
  iptables v1.8.11 (nf_tables)

$ ls -la /usr/sbin/iptables*
  lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root  7 Jan 23 01:35 /usr/sbin/iptables -> xtables
  lrwxrwxrwx. 1 root root 17 Jan 23 01:35 /usr/sbin/iptables-nft -> xtables-nft-multi

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create an EKS cluster with mixed node groups:
    - Node group A: Bottlerocket aws-k8s-1.32 AMI
    - Node group B: Bottlerocket aws-k8s-1.34 AMI
  2. Deploy kube-proxy in iptables mode (EKS default)
  3. Deploy enough services to generate meaningful load (100+ services)
  4. Check kube-proxy logs on both node types:
  kubectl logs -n kube-system <kube-proxy-pod> | grep "SyncProxyRules complete"

```sh
!/bin/bash

# Ensure we are running as root
if [ "$EUID" -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "Please run as root"
  exit 1
fi

RULES_FILE="/tmp/iptables-compare.rules"
TARGET_RULES=300000
OUTER_LOOPS=$((TARGET_RULES / 10))

# Cleanup function to ensure a clean slate between backend tests
cleanup() {
    # Flush both backends explicitly
    /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy -F 2>/dev/null
    /usr/sbin/iptables-legacy -X test 2>/dev/null
    /usr/sbin/iptables-nft -F 2>/dev/null
    /usr/sbin/iptables-nft -X test 2>/dev/null
}

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
echo "1. Generating $TARGET_RULES dummy rules..."
cat << 'EOF' > $RULES_FILE
*filter
:INPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
:test - [0:0]
-A INPUT -j test
-A INPUT -j ACCEPT
EOF

for SCALE in $(seq 1 $OUTER_LOOPS); do
    OCTET3=$((SCALE % 256))
    OCTET2=$(((SCALE / 256) % 256))
    for j in {1..10}; do
        echo "-A test -s 10.$OCTET2.$OCTET3.$j -j DROP" >> $RULES_FILE
    done
done
echo "COMMIT" >> $RULES_FILE

echo "2. Payload generated. File size: $(du -sh $RULES_FILE | cut -f1)"
echo "--------------------------------------------------------"

run_benchmark() {
    BACKEND=$1
    RESTORE_BIN="/usr/sbin/iptables-$BACKEND-restore"
    SAVE_BIN="/usr/sbin/iptables-$BACKEND-save"

echo "--- Testing Backend: $BACKEND ---"
    cleanup
    
    echo ">> Measuring $BACKEND-restore..."
    # Using 'time' with a format for readability
    TIMEFORMAT=$'\tReal: %R\tUser: %U\tSys: %S'
    time $RESTORE_BIN < $RULES_FILE

echo ">> Measuring $BACKEND-save..."
    time $SAVE_BIN > /dev/null
    echo ""
}

rm $RULES_FILE

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
I honestly didn't expect this big performance difference between the iptables-nft and iptables-legacy, the kube-proxy `nftables` backend https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/121046 solves the problem and we tested more exhaustively https://gist.github.com/aojea/f9ca1a51e2afd03621744c95bfdab5b8 , but we still support the `iptables` backend as default.

To document a bit more of the history

@danwinship fixed most of the scale issues with iptables backend when the partial sync was implemented https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/110268

but later we realized we removed the periodic full sync and was readed with a hardcoded time of 1 hour  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/129128

This full sync on large clusters are going to create this "stop the world" pause every hour, impacting users workloads

Talked today during SIG Network meeting and we can expose a generic flag that controls this value, so users can decide to configure it or fully disable it

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/5ed1fd971d594a3cda7f7e26cd037e8be37f520f/pkg/proxy/iptables/proxier.go#L312

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text: Confirmed  most likely the problem is the massive number of netlink calls on the `iptables-nft` vs the `iptables-legacy`, see the time spent in `sendmsg` and `recvmsg`

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
"kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
> I honestly didn't expect this big performance difference between the iptables-nft and iptables-legacy

Basically, iptables-legacy had a decade of performance fixes which iptables-nft lost many of, since it was a major rewrite. And iptables-nft is also unable to benefit from most of the things that make nftables better than iptables, because of the constraints of compatibly implementing the iptables command-line API.

> In parallel we need to talk with netfilter folks to see if we can improve the experience

There have been some fixes to iptables-nft performance, and using more recent releases rather than older 1.8.x releases should help some.

But they'd rather be spending their time improving nftables performance than iptables-nft performance these days. (And we'd rather have them doing that too?)

title: "kube-proxy iptables mode has 5-6x performance regression when switching to iptables-nft backend"
text:
In addition to that, I think that there is a fundamental problem that can not be solved , `iptables-nft` can’t use the features that make nftables fast (like sets and maps) because it’s forced to maintain compatibility with the legacy iptables CLI API.

So my understanding of these tests is that `iptables-legacy` just uploads an entire blob O(1) and swaps it, meanwhile iptables-nft` translate every rule to its netlink representation , and then processes each rule independently O(N).

As we saw with the kube-proxy nftables implementation, the only real way to win is to move to the 'nftables way'—using sets and maps to handle IPs in a single call rather than processing rules one-by-one.

NOTE: just to clarify, this is dataplane programming, time to flush the rules to disk, not dataplane processing ... and is a local node operation, there is no impact on the scalability of the cluster

### What happened?

I tried to install kubeadm following the steps below,  but it failed with the following command.

https://kubernetes.io/docs/setup/production-environment/tools/kubeadm/install-kubeadm/#installing-kubeadm-kubelet-and-kubectl

```
$ curl -fsSL https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.36/deb/Release.key | sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg
curl: (35) error:0A000126:SSL routines::unexpected eof while reading
gpg: no valid OpenPGP data found.
```

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
### What happened?

We list configmaps from an informer like this:

`configMaps, err := informer.Core().V1().ConfigMaps().Lister().ConfigMaps(pod.Namespace).List(labels.Everything())`

And got the following error:

`panic: interface conversion: interface {} is nil, not *v1.ConfigMap

goroutine 13875768 [running]:
k8s.io/client-go/listers.ResourceIndexer[...].List.func1()
	k8s.io/client-go@v0.32.1/listers/generic_helpers.go:51 +0xc5
k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache.ListAllByNamespace({0x4877110, 0xc43b070750}, {0xc1e1e24e70, 0x14}, {0x4862ed0, 0x6e707f0}, 0xc001012820)
	k8s.io/client-go@v0.32.1/tools/cache/listers.go:84 +0x378
k8s.io/client-go/listers.ResourceIndexer[...].List(0x0?, {0x4862ed0, 0x6e707f0})
	k8s.io/client-go@v0.32.1/listers/generic_helpers.go:50`

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
@tskillian can you provide more context on where is this happening and how the informers are being used?
is this code running in an OSS project we can take a look or some chance you can provide a link to some snippet?

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
We have our own provider implementation for [virtual kubelet](https://github.com/virtual-kubelet/virtual-kubelet). The virtual kubelet does setup its own informer for the same type of object (config maps, [here](https://github.com/virtual-kubelet/virtual-kubelet/blob/master/node/nodeutil/controller.go#L346)) as the panic is occurring in. However, the library doesn't expose this informer and we need to be informed about config maps too, so our provider is also setting up a config map informer on the same cluster.

In our case, the panic is coming from when our provider is trying to list all config maps. We basically initialize and wait for sync like this:

`informer := informers.NewSharedInformerFactoryWithOptions(
k8sClient
30*time.Second,
)

informer.Core().V1().ConfigMaps().Informer()
informer.WaitForCacheSync(ctx.Done())
`

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
And then later we are frequently iterating over configmap, fetching them as follows. We go down this code path at least once a minute on average, but sometimes ~100 times in a minute which does seem correlated to when the panic occurs.

`informer.Core().V1().ConfigMaps().Lister().ConfigMaps(pod.Namespace).List(labels.Everything())`

We also have an event handler on config maps, but the panic has only ever come with the above List() call. We have since refactored to be able to use Get() instead of List() and haven't had any panics since then.

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text: I wonder if something is mutating the cache and causing this problem, there is an env variable that you can set `KUBE_CACHE_MUTATION_DETECTOR` that will log mutation of cache objects

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
> We also have an event handler on config maps, but the panic has only ever come with the above List() call.

Do you have a link to what your event handler is doing with the config maps, or what the code was doing with configmaps once listed? Any chance either of those was leading to a code path that set one of those pointers to nil in some way?

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
I got a related error of my projects but in Get, object and error are both nil. This occurs about once every few days causing a panic and then the controller is rebooted.

I've checked all the relevant code and can't find how `nil` is being introduced. I didn't configure any transform and Index, everything is default, the most likely cause is the transformer inside the cache, but I can't find a transformFunc that return a `nil`.

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
Oh, I think I've found the reason. GetIndexers() return to indexes that are being modified, If I understand correctly...
I sent a patch #135440 for fix it.

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
The missing read lock on GetIndexers is a good catch, though I'm trying to understand if it is related to the panic you are seeing...

Are you dynamically adding / removing indexers during the time the panic is seen? Is GetIndexers being invoked on every ListAllByNamespace call?

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
I'm not dynamically adding or removing indexers, so I think that's not the cause of the problem now.

I tried to reproduce it locally today but couldn't.

I'm now changing the dependency to v0.33.6 to see if this issue will still be triggered. It might take a few days.

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text: that hits the race when trying to Add a key

title: Panic in client-go ListAllByNamespace
text:
I've checked the whole project and there is no add/remove on indexers, so this should be ruled out.

I'm changing the dependency to v0.33.6 to see if this issue is still triggered. 
This will help to rule out whether it was introduced in the latest release and narrow it down. This process may take a few days.
