title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
text: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0

title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
text:
### What happened?

After upgrading to Kubernetes 1.36.0, the kubelet crashes directly after startup on systems that have the `/var/lib/kubelet` folder configured on a ZFS filesystem. The direct error shown in the logs is:

```
E0423 21:45:14.682963  326484 kubelet.go:1821] "Failed to start ContainerManager" err="failed to get rootfs info: failed to get device for dir \"/var/lib/kubelet\": with major: 0, minor: 60: could not find device in cached partitions map"
```

This error is caused by the kubelet not being able to determine the available space for empheral storage, which it normally gets using cadvisor.

After increasing the log level, it seems that this is caused by cadvisor not having access to its ZFS plugin:
```
Apr 23 21:45:14 strike01 kubelet[326484]: I0423 21:45:14.680361  326484 fs.go:406] no plugin found for filesystem type: zfs
```

title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

Like Kubernetes 1.35.3 (or 1.35.4 for that matter): I expect the kubelet to startup and work.

title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

As described above: Ensure `/var/lib/kubelet` is on a ZFS filesystem and start the kubelet with any configuration.

title: kubelet does not start when kubelet folder is on ZFS filesystem because of missing cadvisor plugin in 1.36.0
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

I am aware that Kubernetes does not maintain cadvisor and that cadvisor bugs should not be posted here. However, I believe that cadvisor is working correctly, it is just missing its ZFS plugin.

FS plug-ins in cadvisor are introduced in version v0.55.0, before the introduction everything was built-in. Kubernetes 1.36.0 brings cadvisor from version [v0.53.0 → v0.56.0](https://github.com/google/cadvisor/compare/v0.53.0...v0.56.0). It was specifically introduced in https://github.com/google/cadvisor/commit/28ecaa9d9b4aa2ea878f7692a315797c2a9d7a7c

In the PR merging 0.55.1, #135829, I see that some file system extensions are specifically being imported (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135829/changes#diff-ca41d4fa89358cfbf866e71de1c54ab978a448e805495bf8bca923d85dae31fb), but the ZFS plug-in is missing.

title: [pkgs.k8s.io] `cri-tools` and `kubernetes-cni` packages are missing in v1.36 repo
text:
> The following packages have unmet dependencies:
>  kubeadm : Depends: cri-tools (>= 1.30.0) but it is not installable
>  kubelet : Depends: kubernetes-cni (>= 1.2.0) but it is not installable

according to the release tooling (krel) metadata used to populate dependencies, kubeadm >= 1.32.2 has no dependencies:
https://github.com/kubernetes/release/blob/master/cmd/krel/templates/latest/metadata.yaml#L77-L78

but i think the problem there is that there are two `>=` constrains and that's a bug.
i can send a fix to make the `>= 1.30` constraint to be a range.

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
text: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
text:
### What happened?

We're seeing when the senario in [cronjob_controllerv2.go#L615-L637](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L615-L637) happened (Cronjob Controller found an existed job), Cronjob controller will try to get the job without specifying `namespace` value and get HTTP 404:

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
text:
#### kube-controller-manager log:

```
E0210 18:47:10.835604       1 cronjob_controllerv2.go:179] "Unhandled Error" err="error syncing CronJobController default/sleep-300-job, requeuing: the server could not find the requested resource" logger="UnhandledError"
```

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
text:
#### API Server log:

```
{
    "kind": "Event",
    "apiVersion": "audit.k8s.io/v1",
    "level": "RequestResponse",
    "auditID": "db48fd5c-6a37-4639-a6f0-2fcfeb0b5e8b",
    "stage": "ResponseComplete",

# this doesn't contain namespace
    =====================
    "requestURI": "/apis/batch/v1/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485",
    =====================

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
text:
The issue was the "requestURI": `/apis/batch/v1/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485` is correct, it should contain `namespace` e.g.:
```
❯ kubectl get job sleep-300-job-29512485 -v 9                                      
...
I0210 18:52:07.314585   31290 round_trippers.go:632] "Response" verb="GET" url="https://192.168.130.2:8443/apis/batch/v1/namespaces/default/jobs/sleep-300-job-29512485" status="200 OK" headers=<
```

And the API Server's 404 indicated there's no `/apis/batch/v1/jobs/` API.

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

Cronjob controller should query the job `with namespace`:

Incorrect URI (current):
```
/apis/batch/v1/jobs/<JOB_NAME>
```

Correct URI:
```
/apis/batch/v1/namespaces/<NAMESPACE>/jobs/<JOB_NAME>
```

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create a cronjob to create a job every 5 minutes:
```
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: sleep-300-job
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: echo-sleep
              image: ubuntu
              command:
                - /bin/bash
                - -c
                - echo start && sleep 180 && echo end
          restartPolicy: Never
```

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
Once the first job was created, create another job manually with the next expected job name (id + 5):
```
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: sleep-300-job-29512465
  namespace: default
  ownerReferences:
  - apiVersion: batch/v1
    blockOwnerDeletion: true
    controller: true
    kind: CronJob
    name: sleep-300-job
    uid: d10ff40f-be9d-47d2-aa67-7a376f054ed2
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - command:
        - /bin/bash
        - -c
        - echo start && sleep 180 && echo end
        image: ubuntu
        name: echo-sleep
      restartPolicy: Never
```

Check the kube-controller-manager and API Server audit log for the result.

title: Cronjob controller try to get existed job without namespace
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

The issue seems to be when Cronjob controller trying to get job, it used the `jobReq` from `getJobFromTemplate2()` function, however, `getJobFromTemplate2()` won't set the job Namespace:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L621
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L604
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/pkg/controller/cronjob/utils.go#L256-L263

Seems we're not relying on the `namespace` from `getJobFromTemplate2()`, it seems the correct way to specify `namespace` should be the same as:
* https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/65f09e605cb206b2e5fcff4d69a4ae8acf62dbc3/pkg/controller/cronjob/cronjob_controllerv2.go#L609

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
text: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
text:
### What happened?

In the `syncProxyRules` logic of kube-proxy's IPVS mode, during the initial sync phase, the weight of each RealServer is updated if **any** of the existing destinations (`curDests`) has a different weight from the newly created `newDest`. This logic may lead to unnecessary or even incorrect updates.

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
text:
#### Why it's problematic:

The current code updates `newDest` without checking if the existing `dest` in `curDests` actually matches the IP and port of `newDest`. This can result in:
- Unnecessary repeated updates to the same RealServer.
- Updates triggered by unrelated `dest` entries.
- Inefficient syncing behavior during startup.

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

The logic should compare the `Address` and `Port` of `dest` and `newDest`, and only perform an update if:
```go
dest.Address.Equal(newDest.Address) && dest.Port == newDest.Port && dest.Weight != newDest.Weight
```

This ensures only the correct RealServer is updated when needed.

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

This logic runs during the initial sync phase. In environments with multiple RealServers with varying weights, the current code may repeatedly trigger `UpdateRealServer` on unrelated destinations.

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

The logic should compare the `Address` and `Port` of `dest` and `newDest`, and only perform an update if:
```go
dest.Address.Equal(newDest.Address) && dest.Port == newDest.Port && dest.Weight != newDest.Weight
```

This ensures only the correct RealServer is updated when needed.

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
text:
### Kubernetes version

- Kubernetes version: [ v1.29.0]
- IPVS mode enabled

title: Potential incorrect RealServer weight update logic in IPVS proxier initial sync
text:
Hi @danwinship 👋,

Thanks for accepting the issue.

Before I proceed with submitting the PR, I would like to double-check the intended behavior here to avoid any misunderstanding.

The original comment says:

```go
// if we are syncing for the first time, loop through all current destinations and
// reset their weight.
```

At first glance, this suggests resetting the weight of **all** RealServers during the initial sync.  
However, the actual logic checks whether a RealServer matches the newly created `newDest` (by IP and Port) and only updates if the weight differs.

My understanding is that the code is intended to **only reset the weight for the matching destination**, not for all destinations.  
Is that correct?

If yes, then after successfully finding and updating the matching destination, would it make sense to `break` out of the loop immediately to avoid unnecessary iterations?

Example updated logic:

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

This is a feature request for Kubernetes to resize memory-backed volumes without container restarts, similar to in-place pod resizing for CPU / memory.

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text:
### Why is this needed?

Ray project is working on an integration to use kuebrnetes in-place pod resizing in the Ray autoscaler. When upsizing memory in-place, we need to also resize the memory backed volume Ray uses for it's object store. This object store is typically configured to use ~30% of the memory requests in the Pod. Ray autoscaler can be responsible for setting the size but needs the ability to resize memory-backed volumes without restarts.

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text: Thanks @andrewsykim, for bringing this up!

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text: @rueian Would you be able to share more about the priority of this feature for the Ray Autoscaler? Is it a blocker? If so, can you provide a little more details about your use case, any workarounds you have at the moment, and what your required timeline is for us to support this?

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text:
Hi @natasha41575  and @HirazawaUi,

Ray uses a memory object store backed by `/dev/shm` to store and share arguments and results for remote functions (ray tasks). Users usually configure their `/dev/shm` in this way:

```yaml
volumes:
  - name: dshm
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory
      sizeLimit: 4Gi
```

Currently, the IPPR integration in the Ray autoscaler will only adjust the memory request/limit of the container and leave the object store untouched, but we also want to allow users to resize the object store as well, allowing users to store more in the object store, which means resizing the memory volume is a requirement.

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text:
/triage accepted

I plan to tackle this in 1.37

title: Support dynamically resizing memory backed volumes
text:
I discussed with SIG Node and SIG Storage. Both are supportive of moving forward with a KEP, targeting an alpha in 1.37.

I opened a tracking issue for the feature in k/enhancements: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/6030. We can track the progress there, and close this issue.

/close

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text: Add validation around Pod IP Address

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Today we make assumptions around the Pod IP address coming back from the PodSandboxStatus RPC. This RPC is responsible for returning the ip addresses to the kubelet. The assumptions we make around the current handling of pod ip addresses from containerd and crio, while they do some validation if the pod ip address exists, it possible to return an empty string as the pod ip address. It probably makes sense to do validation in the SyncPod function (link below) to ensure this scenario cannot happen. The function should fail, right now the pod is constantly scheduled without a useful error message. 

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/4b5e40aacd021aa1485d29c4965893b3de017697/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L1596

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
### Why is this needed?

Without any validation, its possible for a pod to come up and have no ip address from the K8s perspective.

text: /assign @thesauravpoddar

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
> ### Why is this needed?
> Without any validation, its possible for a pod to come up and have no ip address from the K8s perspective.

I assume that this relies on the containter runtime to have the convention of always return the ipaddresses values, but I'm curious how far this goes, the PodIP plumbing through the kubelet is a nice mace , I wonder if at some point the fact that a Network Pod does not have any Pod IP fails one of the existing kubelet logic (probes come to mind) or just leaves a Pod without IPs running on the cluster ... @MikeZappa87 have you prototyped this?

/cc @bowei @danwinship @thockin

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text: @aojea I can put a quick prototype together again to verify, however if I remember correctly, the pod stayed up. I didn't test health checks or anything. The eth0 in the pod network namespace did have an ip address assigned to it, however since the PodSandboxStatus didn't return any ip addresses the kubelet was just unaware. Let me iron out the wrinkles here. If we need to fix this, we should just cut it off at the source and just focus on the link I sent any anything that calls PodSandboxStatus, the paths for the pod ip goes deep.

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text: Usually is on the people working on the issue  to define the problem and the solution , and also provide tests to validate your solution

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
If PodSandboxStatus returns "" or " ", the issue does not happen however if you return a string such as "broken" the pods can come up. If the pods have a readiness probe like CoreDNS the pod is still running but not ready. We should not make assumptions about the cri-api server details and should enforce validation.

Link to code change to demostrate:
https://github.com/containerd/containerd/compare/main...MikeZappa87:containerd:mzappa/missingpodip

readiness probe:
kube-system          coredns-7d764666f9-kxts5                         0/1     Running   1 (100s ago)   3m37s   <none>

No probe:
local-path-storage   local-path-provisioner-67b8995b4b-nvk4t          1/1     Running   0              3m37s   <none>

It probably makes sense to add additional validation here:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b6eb3d0546de9261160641d9dd8cb92139cdfe81/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/util/util.go#L63

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text: It could be as simple as a simple boolean that validates the ipv4/ipv6 address. This should prevent the kubelet from executing the Create/StartContainer RPC's since the pod has no valid ip addresses. The eth0 does have an ip address however its unknown to K8s which probably still violates 'every pod needs to have an ip address'

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
why the container runtime should not validate the ips? it looks to me that the runtime should never send this data ,

https://github.com/containerd/containerd/blob/35871e04ab5fc51b1411c44d203346d6e9a587dc/internal/cri/server/sandbox_run.go#L456-L467

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text: We should not assume the cri is doing the appropriate validation. We only know about two runtimes. This is pretty much a net.ParseIp addition to verify the field is in fact a ip address (v4/V6).

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
/sig node

I'm not sure how much validation of PodSandboxStatus kubelet currently does. It seems plausible to argue that if the runtime gives kubelet garbage out-of-spec values, then kubelet is allowed to behave in a garbage out-of-spec way.

title: Add validation around Pod IP Address
text:
The PodSandboxStatus is used in a couple of places. Some of the validation that uses the response checks for not empty string and using a net.ParseIp function in the code.

This PodSandboxStatus in the first usage of the RPC as its part of the pod creation (RunPodSandbox, PodSandboxStatus, Create/StartContainer). Link below.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/574b3aad0fae8ab3efea5ec4767567e9e5bb21dc/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L1568

Adding some validation right here would cause the pod to never fully come up since it would trigger the reconciliation. Validation can be added elsewhere as well.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/b6eb3d0546de9261160641d9dd8cb92139cdfe81/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/util/util.go#L63

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/574b3aad0fae8ab3efea5ec4767567e9e5bb21dc/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L1599

Curious what sig-node provides here.

title: [DRA] pod.status.resourceClaimStatuses entries are flapping
text:
### What happened?

I have a Pod that uses two ResourceClaimTemplates that is stuck in pending for various (irrelevant) reasons. I noticed that the status.resourceClaimStatuses field was flapping between entries with the the RCs created for each of the templates:

```console

vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286634832495008   model
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286634891151008   gpu
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286634934959008   model
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286634986255008   gpu
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635035711008   model
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635087583008   gpu
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635134271008   model
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635186143008   gpu
vllm-opt-125m-6b6f655ddb-8zb4p   1776286635235007008   model
```

This shouldn't be happening. Once the resource claims are created, they should be added there and it should remain stable.

text:
### What did you expect to happen?

Once the resource claims are created, they should be added there and it should remain stable. Here's an example of a Pod that has reached `Running` state:

```console

I suspect this happens whenever a pod is in pending and uses multiple ResourceClaims, but I am not sure. I have a unit test that reproduces it, but not an e2e test. I will submit a PR with the unit test and a fix.

title: KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata
text: KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata

title: KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata
text:
### What happened?

When count>1 is used in resource claim spec and requests are allocated with different drivers, the consumer-side readRequestDir merge in k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation/devicemetadata produces duplicate DeviceMetadataRequest entries with the same Name rather than a single merged entry. Xref: https://github.com/kubernetes/dynamic-resource-allocation/blob/c46864948a5f87b6ba006b6823a6893cbe2bf064/devicemetadata/decode.go#L150-L153

title: KEP-5304: requests with count>1 produce incorrect device metadata
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

For this case a single entry in DeviceMetadata.Requests was expected.

Each entry in DeviceMetadata.Requests should be with a unique name. When the same request is used to allocate multiple devices potentially from different drivers, the devices should be merged into a single request.

Once it is fixed consumer code should: (as far as i understand)
1. find the request by name
2. iterate devices (unless n == N, n required, N exists)
3. if there is more than one, pick the device(s) whose driver / attributes / networkData match what it needs
(unless all have the same set)

title: NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST
text:
NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST

This feature is currently off-by-default, but it's slated for GA in 1.36 https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/2258

Recently we discovered that while GET works at head POST stopped working after the project upgraded to go 1.26
See previously: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137459

The handler for this API should probably be evaluated before it goes GA.

/kind bug
/sig node

At the very least, it was either unintentionally working before. The handler should probably restrict verbs explicitly.

title: NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST
text: It would also be good to understand why this stopped working, in case it impacts any other APIs.

title: NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST
text: Taking a look, I [commented](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/137459#issuecomment-4013357373) on the parent issue.

title: NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST
text:
> Is it confirmed that the "stopped accepting POST" is exclusive to the [query](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137462/files#diff-9b2eaad633b1ac2ad543e05ddeb831f207bfb4d034456070bc7d32ea45b7ed05R624) feature, or is this a behavior inherited from the [logs](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137462/files#diff-9b2eaad633b1ac2ad543e05ddeb831f207bfb4d034456070bc7d32ea45b7ed05L623) endpoint

Unclear, the primary concern at the time was unbreaking the data race signal, but I'm concerned that we don't know why this happened, it's entirely possible it's not specific to either of these endpoints, but I think it's less likely with GA endpoints.

title: NodeLogQuery stopped accepting POST
text:
/triage accepted
/priority important-longterm

/assign @amritansh1502

(@amritansh1502 has a PR for it)

title: kube-apiserver log spammed with "failed to connect to etcd"
text:
### What happened?

Running 1.34.1 with talos 1.11.0 (etcd 3.6.4). It doesn't seem to be related to talos.

All kube-apiserver logs are spammed every 15sec with
```
grpc: addrConn.createTransport failed to connect to {Addr: "localhost:2379", ServerName: "localhost:2379", BalancerAttributes: {"<%!p(pickfirstleaf.managedByPickfirstKeyType={})>": "<%!p(bool=true)>" }}. Err: connection error: desc = "transport: Error while dialing: dial tcp: lookup localhost: operation was canceled"
```

The cluster is working as expected.

See also https://github.com/siderolabs/talos/issues/11797

title: kube-apiserver log spammed with "failed to connect to etcd"

kube-apiserver log spammed with "failed to connect to etcd"
text:
>@RomRider! I'm seeing the same issue on v1.34.1 - the every-15-second "failed to connect to etcd" warnings are definitely noise when the cluster is functioning normally.
>
>@ovstuckrad's verbose logs show this is happening during gRPC connection lifecycle management, where canceled operations during address resolution are generating warnings unnecessarily.
>
>This seems like a logging level issue where normal connection management events (like canceled DNS lookups during connection switching) are being logged as warnings instead of debug/info level.
>
>Quick question: Are you seeing this with external etcd or embedded etcd? The localhost:2379 in the original report vs the etcd-0.etcd.namespace.svc.cluster.local.:2379 in @ovstuckrad's logs suggests it might affect both configurations.
>
>Since the cluster is working fine, this appears to be purely a logging noise issue that could be addressed by adjusting the log level for these specific gRPC connection events.
>
>/sig api-machinery looks correct - this is de

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
[declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)

Declarative validation's `validation-gen`'s `ParseInt` returns Go `int` (64-bit) but does not validate that the parsed value fits in the target field's type. Values outside the target type's range are silently accepted at codegen time. For unsigned types, large values cannot be parsed at all. No boundary condition tests exist.

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
### Impact

**int32 vs int64:** All Kubernetes API integers are `int32` or `int64`. Writing `+k8s:minimum=-2147483649` on an `int32` field passes codegen without error but emits a literal that overflows `int32`.

**Unsigned types:** `strconv.Atoi` cannot parse values above `math.MaxInt64`, so `+k8s:maximum=18446744073709551615` on a `uint64` field fails. No kubernets native API types use unsigned integers today (from looking through `*/types.go` files), so this would be for library correctness currently.

**Untyped literal emission:** `toGolangSourceDataLiteral` uses `fmt.Sprintf("%v")` which emits untyped literals that may overflow or truncate near type boundaries.

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
### How to reproduce

1. Add `+k8s:minimum=-2147483649` to an `int32` field on a type with validation-gen enabled.
2. Run `hack/update-codegen.sh`.
3. Codegen succeeds, but the generated literal overflows `int32`.

Or:

1. Add `+k8s:maximum=18446744073709551615` to a `uint64` field.
2. Run `hack/update-codegen.sh`.
3. Codegen fails: `strconv.Atoi` cannot parse the value.

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
### What should happen

Codegen should reject values outside the target type's range with a clear error (e.g., "value -2147483649 does not fit in int32"). Values at the boundary (e.g., `-2147483648` for int32) should work correctly and emit properly typed Go literals.

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
### Fix outline

1. Split `ParseInt` into `ParseSignedInt` (returns `int64`) and `ParseUnsignedInt` (returns `uint64`).
2. In `minimumTagValidator`/`maximumTagValidator`, check the field's signedness, call the right parser, and validate the value fits the target type's range.
3. Emit typed literals (`int64(...)`, `uint64(...)`) in `toGolangSourceDataLiteral`.
4. Add boundary condition tests for int32 min/max, int64 min/max, uint32 max, uint64 max, and out-of-range rejection.

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text:
### Related PR // Background

Discovered via [PR #136589](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136589). @thockin noted from the PR there this issue as the PR there - specified +k8s:ifEnabled("WorkloadAwarePreemption")=+k8s:minimum=-2147483648 based on our guidance that all ints should spec min and max.

/sig api-machinery
/kind bug

title: [declarative validation] `+k8s:minimum` / `+k8s:maximum` do not correctly handle signedness and associated type bounds/boundary-values (ex: int32 vs int64)
text: /cc @thockin @jpbetz @yongruilin @lalitc375

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
### What happened?

When operating in environments with high pod churn rates—where pods are frequently created and terminated—and multiple services are configured to reference a single deployment, we observed that network connectivity on some of the pods is failing on windows nodes.

On the pods where we have network issue, tried to do DNS lookup by exec into the pods and it failed to resolve the DNS.

Attached are the logs on two windows nodes

[9a98.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727718/9a98.zip)

[bf83.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25727725/bf83.zip)

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

Network connectivity shouldn't break on windows pods and DNS resolution should succeed.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1.  Deploy kubernetes cluster with limited available IPs for pods on both Linux and Windows node.
2. Deploy Linux pods that consume almost all IPs.
3. Deploy service type LoadBalancer that refer to Linux pods, This will trigger kube-proxy to create Remote HNS endpoint on Windows. This Remote HNS endpoint will have Linux pod IP.
4. Scale down the Linux pods to 1.
5. Do multiple time scaling up and down.
6. Deploy windows deployment with multiple replicas and have multiple service referencing the deployment.
7. Scale down the windows pods to 1.
8. Perform the same operation multiple time (scaling up and down)
9. After few days we see that on few of the pods, if we do the nslookup, it fails to connect and resolve DNS.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

I was going through the below PR and want to check whether any regression caused the network connectivity issue. 
PR1: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135146
PR2: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133953
All our customers reported that, they are seeing intermittent network connectivity issues on K8S >1.30.

Realistically: It is unlikely that any maintainers of Windows or Networking have sufficient spare time without more specifics that clearly point to a Kubernetes bug, that just isn't sustainable.

text: We require evidence that the issue exists on a current version since we can't patch older releases anyhow (your vendor could in their fork if they wish) and have merged many improvements since that time, which might've already fixed any upstream bug if there was one.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
Based on my review, none of the PRs you mentioned appear to be the cause of this regression. [PR1 ](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/135146) was not backported to 1.31, and since the issue is occurring on 1.31 as well, it is unlikely to be related. [PR2 ](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/133953) was backported to 1.31, but its changes do not impact LoadBalancer creation.

From the logs you shared, I can see that DNS HNS policies are present on both nodes. However, one difference I noticed is that the DSR flag does not appear to be set in the logs. This could potentially be the reason for the issue, as it may prevent the VFP load balancer rules from being installed.

Additionally, the logs you shared did not include kubeclusterconfig.json or kubeproxy error logs. I suspect that in the newer release deployments, one of the required flags may not have been set. In particular:

`--enable-dsr="true"` (while starting Windows kube-proxy)

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
`--feature-gates="WinDSR=true,WinOverlay=false"` (configured in kubeclusterconfig.json)

Could you please check the kube-proxy error logs (especially near the beginning) and confirm whether you see log entries similar to the following?

```
I0408 06:42:24.113666    7016 flags.go:64] FLAG: --enable-dsr="true"
I0408 06:42:24.113666    7016 flags.go:64] FLAG: --feature-gates="WinDSR=true,WinOverlay=false"
```

This should help confirm whether the required flags are being set correctly.

text: Not sure whether the DSR flag is causing any intermittent network issues on the nodes but curious how DNS resolution on few pods succeeds without DSR flag enabled and fails on few.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
Based on the information shared, I compared the pattern of DNS VFP rules between the working node and the node you provided. It appears that the LB DSR rules are missing on the affected node. Typically, these rules are added when the isdsr flag is set in the HNS policy, which seems to be absent in this case.

Could you please share the packet traces collected using c:\k\debug\startpacketcapture.ps1, along with the VFP rules captured during both the times when DNS is working and when it is not? It is possible that I may be overlooking something, or that the logs shared earlier were not captured during the working timeframe. Having this additional information would help me better understand the situation.

I also noticed some additional DNS rules on the container NIC port. I’m not certain whether they might be contributing to the DNS behavior (working vs. non-working), but it would be helpful to review them further.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text: Additionally, were you able to confirm whether the enabledsr configuration was in use prior to version 1.31? If possible, could you also try enabling this configuration and check whether the issue still persists? As per the HNS Policy and VFP rules, the core dns pod ips are 192.168.7.41 and 192.168.7.52. I assume they are right ipaddress of the dns pods. Thank you, and I appreciate your help in gathering this information. /assign @princepereira

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
@smandhada16 ,

Based on the details you shared, there are two CoreDNS pods: 192.168.7.38 (coredns-6b7fdfbc95-zfhv2) and 192.168.7.62 (coredns-6b7fdfbc95-4v4n9). These same pods are also configured as backend DIPs in the HNS policy as well as in the VFP load balancer rules.
Since the expected DNS IP addresses are correctly configured in the HNS policies and VFP rules that you shared, we can reasonably exclude the earlier suspected PRs as the root cause.

When the VFP port performs backend selection for DNS queries, some requests are directed to 192.168.7.38, while others are routed to 192.168.7.62. From the observations so far, DNS queries sent to 192.168.7.38 are consistently resolved and return valid responses. However, queries that are routed to 192.168.7.62 appear to leave the node but do not receive a response afterward. This could potentially indicate an issue either with the network path or with the DNS pod itself.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text: The intermittent DNS success you are observing likely corresponds to cases where the backend selection routes the request to 192.168.7.38, while the failures occur when 192.168.7.62 is selected as the backend.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
@princepereira, 
When I tried exec into pods where I was seeing the issue and do explicit nslookup against CoreDNS pods, it succeeds. Also, I tried with Test-NetConnection to both CoreDNS pods, it succeeded.

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
Something I noticed is CoreDNS pods are restarting every 24 hrs due to system reboots and gets a new IP.
On the windows nodes, I see kube-proxy is creating new HNS remote endpoints with that IP, then I scaled up new pods to check the nslookup by exec into the pods. Here is what I observed.
- All the pods connectivity succeeds except one pod.
- DNS lookup succeeds if I explicitly test with the CoreDNS IP's but the `Test-NetConnection 10.100.0.10 -Port 53` with the DNSServerAddress fails.
- Verified other pod which landed on the same node and DNS lookup succeeds without any issues.
- Something interesting what I observed is, for the pod where I'm seeing an issue there is a remote HNS stale endpoint which didn't get cleaned up.
- I tried multiple iteration of scaling up and down, seeing issues on pod with same IP address where there is a stale remote HNS endpoint on the node.
- To mitigate the issue, I deleted the stale HNS endpoint and then scaled the deployment. Now the pod with IP where I saw issue previously

title: Windows: Seeing intermittent DNS issues on k8s > 1.30
text:
@smandhada16 ,

Your observation looks correct. A stale remote endpoint is causing VFP to prefer the remote endpoint’s MAC address over the local one, which results in packets being encapsulated and sent to a different node.

I’ve implemented a fix to prevent multiple endpoints from existing with the same IP (shared over slack). It would be really helpful if you could test this using the shared kube-proxy binary. The changes are based on the 1.33 release, since your testing was also done on that version.

Thanks,

title: Some goroutine leaks in staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache
text:
Hi, I've opened a PR to address the goroutine leaks reported here: #137970

Based on the discussion, since context support was added to `tools/cache` via #126387, the test can now properly shut down informers via context cancellation.

The fix uses `context.WithCancel` + `defer cancel()` and `defer informerFactory.Shutdown()` / `defer ec.queue.ShutDown()` to clean up all leaked goroutines. I also added `goleak.VerifyTestMain` to catch future regressions.

Would appreciate any feedback!

title: Some goroutine leaks in staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache
text: The description of the issue seems inaccurate now, we have no leaks in the actual informer code right? This is a test only inaccuracy where we're not cleaning up the goroutines properly. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137970 handles one test, but we have a lot more improper shutdown cases in the rest of `pkg/controller`.

title: Some goroutine leaks in staging/src/k8s.io/client-go/tools/cache
text:
@Jefftree That's a fair point -- the actual informer code doesn't leak; it's the tests not cleaning up properly.

My PR (#137970) starts with the ephemeral controller test since it was directly referenced in this issue, but the same pattern (missing context cancellation and informer/queue shutdown) exists across many other controller tests in `pkg/controller`. Happy to follow up with additional PRs for those if this approach looks good.

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
### What happened?

I decided to open this issue after investigating https://github.com/stackabletech/trino-operator/issues/854.

The high level description of the problem is that the stateful set controller doesn't update the pods according to the latest STS revision and the pods are left crashlooping **if the first revision is faulty** and can never become available. This doesn't happen if the `MaxUnavailableStatefulSet` feature is disabled.

Unfortunately I cannot create a much simpler example, but I attach the relevant controller log messages below.

Based on these logs you can see that:

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
1. The Stackable Operator for Trino (`trino-op` from now on) creates two `StatefulSet` objects with a single replica. They both expose the same problem, so for simplicity, I will use only one of them from now on (`trino-coordinator-default`).
2. The initial revision of this sts is `trino-coordinator-default-5f5598d4c8`. This first STS  revision has a faulty command args  and can never become available.
3. The `trino-op` updates the sts rapidly to the revision `trino-coordinator-default-787556ff59`. Now the STS command args are correct and the pods should be able to become ready.
4. The pod created initially is never updated so it never becomes ready.

To summarize my understanding, the sts revision is never updated if the pod never becomes ready, and the pod doesn't become ready because it has an old revision.

In the logs you see that `currentRevision` is never the same as `updateRevision`:

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
```
I0304 15: 25:06.514308       1 stateful_set_control.go: 146]"StatefulSet revisions" statefulSet = "kuttl-test-distinct-turtle/trino-coordinator-default" currentRevision = "trino-coordinator-default-5f5598d4c8" updateRevision = "trino-coordinator-default-787556ff59"
```

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

The pod should become ready according to the `updateRevision` of the sts.
Again, this was indeed the case until `MaxUnavailableStatefulSet` feature gate was enabled.

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
I diffed the controller logs between 1.34 (where the bug is not present) and 1.35 (where the bug is present) and what I see is that 1.35 just doesn't update the Pod between "StatefulSet has unavailable Pods" and "Updated Status" whereas 1.34 does.

In fact, I never see this entry in the (1.35) logs: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a0e5f1aba53a16db2c2cd0a2cb33c60f43e4d984/pkg/controller/statefulset/stateful_set_control.go#L732

Here are the relevant snippets:

On 1.34:

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
```
stateful_set_control.go:605] "StatefulSet has unavailable Pods" logger="statefulset-controller" statefulSet="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default" unavailableReplicas=1 pod="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default-0"
stateful_set_control.go:697] "StatefulSet is waiting for Pod to update" logger="statefulset-controller" statefulSet="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default" pod="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default-0"
stateful_set_control.go:121] "Updated status" logger="statefulset-controller" statefulSet="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default" replicas=1 readyReplicas=0 currentReplicas=0 updatedReplicas=1
```

On 1.35:

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
```
stateful_set_control.go:641] "StatefulSet has unavailable Pods" statefulSet="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default" unavailableReplicas=1 pod="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default-0"
stateful_set_control.go:129] "Updated status" statefulSet="kuttl-test-logical-bulldog/trino-coordinator-default" replicas=1 readyReplicas=0 currentReplicas=1 updatedReplicas=0
```

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
I'm not sure if this is the correct fix, but the patch below solves the problem for us.

The problem was two fold:

1. The pods are not updated when unavailablePods==maxUnavailable but they can also never become available in that revision.
2. Seems there is a corner case when the STS only has one replica, in which case the Pod is also not updated.

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
```diff
diff --git a/pkg/controller/statefulset/stateful_set_control.go b/pkg/controller/statefulset/stateful_set_control.go
index dd339cc7814..94eef25cb8d 100644
--- a/pkg/controller/statefulset/stateful_set_control.go
+++ b/pkg/controller/statefulset/stateful_set_control.go
@@ -772,21 +772,26 @@ func updateStatefulSetAfterInvariantEstablished(ctx context.Context, ssc *defaul
 	}
 	metrics.UnavailableReplicas.WithLabelValues(set.Namespace, set.Name, podManagementPolicy).Set(float64(unavailablePods))
 
-	if unavailablePods >= maxUnavailable {
-		// log only when a true violation occurs.
-		if unavailablePods > maxUnavailable {
-			logger.V(4).Info("StatefulSet found unavailablePods, more than the allowed maxUnavailable",
-				"statefulSet", klog.KObj(set),
-				"unavailablePods", unavailablePods,
-				"maxUnavailable", maxUnavailable)
-		}
+	logger.V(4).Info("StatefulSet update checking for unavailable Pods",
+		"statefulSet", klog.KObj(set),
+		"unavailablePods", unavailablePods,
+		"maxUnavailable", maxUnav

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text: Got the most minimal reproducer. The main part is the `.spec.podManagementPolicy`. This problem happens ONLY for `Parallel`, which is expected to recover a pod. Here's a simple StatefulSet with a Service:

title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: sts
spec:
  replicas: 1
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      baz: blah
      foo: bar
  serviceName: svc
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        baz: blah
        foo: bar
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webserver
        args:
        - test-webserver1
        image: registry.k8s.io/e2e-test-images/agnhost:2.55
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: svc
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  clusterIPs:
  - None
  internalTrafficPolicy: Cluster
  ipFamilies:
  - IPv4
  ipFamilyPolicy: SingleStack
  ports:
  - name: http
    port: 80
    protocol: TCP
    targetPort: 80
  selector:
    baz: blah
    foo: bar
  sessionAffinity: None
  type: ClusterIP
```
Now you can trigger the problem running `kubectl apply -f test-sts.yaml ; sed -i "s/test-webserver1/test-webserver/g" test-sts.yaml; sleep 3 ; kubectl apply -f test-sts.yaml`. With k8s 1.34.3 this will recover

text: A quick update, during reviwing https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137666 we've found a few additional edge cases that we'd like to take time and resolve rather than rush with a solution. This means that landing this fix will take some additional time, and because this is an optional functionality we've decided NOT to disable the feature gate, but continue with it as is. title: StatefulSet pod revisions not updated when MaxUnavailableStatefulSet enabled
text:
> because this is an optional functionality we've decided NOT to disable the feature gate, but continue with it as is. This bug is definitely a blocker for GA promotion of [kubernetes/enhancements#961](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/961)

hmm... StatefulSets have been able to use Parallel since 2017 (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/44899). Just because Parallel is not the default doesn't really mean it's ok to regress it due to a newly-enabled maxUnavailable beta feature, especially when the StatefulSet didn't even ask to use the new maxUnavailable field. This still seems disable-worthy in 1.35+ to me until we get a solid fix we're confident in for the maxUnavailable code paths

title: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime
text: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime

title: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime
text:
### What happened?

When creating a Deployment or StatefulSet with an ImageVolume that has an empty `image.reference` field, the API validation passes, but Pod creation fails at runtime. This creates a confusing user experience where workloads appear to be created successfully but never produce running Pods

**Direct Pod creation correctly rejects empty reference:**

```
$ k apply -f pod-with-empty-ref.yaml
The Pod "test" is invalid: spec.volumes[0].image.reference: Required value
```

**But Deployment/StatefulSet incorrectly accepts it:**

```
$ k apply -f deployment-with-empty-ref.yaml
deployment.apps/test-imagevolume-empty-ref created  # Should have been rejected
```

The error only appears in Events when the ReplicaSet controller tries to create Pods:

```
Warning  FailedCreate  replicaset-controller  Error creating: Pod "..." is invalid:
  [spec.volumes[0].image.reference: Required value, ...]
```

title: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

API validation should reject empty `image.reference` in Pod templates (Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, etc.), not just in direct Pod creation.

title: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a kind cluster with Kubernetes v1.35+:

```bash
$ kind create cluster --name test
```

2. Apply this Deployment:

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: test-imagevolume-empty-ref
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: test
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: test
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: test
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ["sleep", "3600"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: img-vol
          mountPath: /data
      volumes:
      - name: img-vol
        image:
          reference: ""  # Empty
          pullPolicy: Always
```

3. Observe

```bash
$ k get deployment
NAME                         READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE
test-imagevolume-empty-ref   0/1     0            0           # Never becomes ready

title: ImageVolume: Empty image.reference in Pod templates passes API validation but fails at runtime
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
$ k describe rs -l app=test | grep -A5 Events
Events:
  Warning  FailedCreate  Error creating: Pod "..." is invalid:
    [spec.volumes[0].image.reference: Required value, ...]
```

title: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist
text: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist

title: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

When multiple StorageClasses are annotated as default (`storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"`), Kubernetes uses the most recently created one as the actual default applied to PVCs without an explicit storageClassName.
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/storage-classes/#default-storageclass

However, kubectl get storageclass currently shows all of them simply as (default), which makes it unclear which StorageClass will actually be used.

I propose enhancing the kubectl get storageclass output to indicate which default StorageClass is effective.

For example:
```
NAME                       PROVISIONER                   RECLAIMPOLICY   AGE
nfs-client (default, effective)   k8s-sigs.io/nfs-...  Delete          3s
standard   (default, inactive)    rancher.io/local-path Delete         48m
```

title: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
or by adding a new column:
```
NAME         PROVISIONER                   DEFAULT   EFFECTIVE   AGE
nfs-client   k8s-sigs.io/nfs-...           true      true        3s
standard     rancher.io/local-path         true      false       48m
```

now display
```
NAME                    PROVISIONER                RECLAIMPOLICY   AGE
nfs-client (default)    k8s-sigs.io/nfs-...        Delete          3s
standard   (default)    rancher.io/local-path      Delete          48m
```

title: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist
text:
### Why is this needed?

This improves user experience and reduces operational mistakes.

title: kubectl get storageclass should indicate the effective default StorageClass when multiple defaults exist
text:
> [@soltysh](https://github.com/soltysh) What do you think about this proposal? when u have time, ptal :)

Basically what Marly is saying, we want to present only single `(default)` value based on that [link provided](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/storage/storage-classes/#default-storageclass), so the output should look like this:
```
NAME                       PROVISIONER                   RECLAIMPOLICY   AGE
nfs-client (default)		k8s-sigs.io/nfs-...			Delete          3s
standard					rancher.io/local-path		Delete         	48m
```
Notices only single `(default)` value, even though multiple storageclasses have the default annotation.

Improve efficiency of requeuing unschedulable pods when a pod is added for gang scheduling
text: Improve efficiency of requeuing unschedulable pods when a pod is added for gang scheduling

title: Improve efficiency of requeuing unschedulable pods when a pod is added for gang scheduling
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

With cluster events, we need to check whether we can requeue unschedulable pods into active queue or backoff queue more efficiently.

/sig scheduling
/assign
/cc @macsko

title: Improve efficiency of requeuing unschedulable pods when a pod is added for gang scheduling
text:
### Why is this needed?

When it comes to gang scheduling, when Pod/Add event happens, it should also check unschedulable pods (because the newly added pod, along with unschedulable pods grouped together may complete the gang/workload).

Workaround was introduced is to use "MoveAllToActiveOrBackoffQueue()" inside the "Add/Pod" cluster event. But this would run queuing hints for all plugins subscribing to such event, while we are only concerned with "GangScheduling" plugin for this particular case.

title: Improve efficiency of requeuing unschedulable pods when a pod is added for gang scheduling
text:
### References

A relevant issue was mentioned earlier in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/127290 and has pull request that wasn't merged/accepted https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/127387 because it adds many lines of code and there was no strong reason to do so.

But with gang scheduling being introduced, this seems to be beneficial.

text:
[Umbrella] KEP-5832 / KEP-4671: Decouple PodGroup API & Workload API Alpha2 for v1.36

This issue tracks the implementation work for v1.36 spanning two related KEPs:

- KEP-5832: Decouple PodGroup API from Workload ([k/enhancements KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/5832-decouple-podgroup-api))
- KEP-4671: Gang Scheduling Workload API v1alpha2 updates ([k/enhancements KEP](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/tree/master/keps/sig-scheduling/4671-gang-scheduling))

title: [Umbrella] KEP-5832 / KEP-4671: Decouple PodGroup API & Workload API Alpha2 for v1.36
text:
**Work Items**
- [x] API Changes (#136976)
    - Create standalone PodGroup API 
    - Update Pod API
    - Refactor Workload spec , API version change (v1alpha1 → v1alpha2)
 - [x] Add declarative validation for `PodGroup` resource and update the `Workload` validation (#135164)
- [x] Add admission plugin that verifies Workload referenced by a PodGroup exists (#137464)
- [x] Add protection controller for PodGroup (#137641)
- [x] PodGroup status updates by kube-scheduler (#137611)
- [x] Scheduler Changes (#136976)
    - Update WorkloadManager updates
    - Update GangScheduling plugin for new API shape and add PodGroup Informer
- [x] Testing (#136976)
    - Update existing integration tests for decoupled API shape
    - Add integration tests for PodGroup lifecycle independent of Workload
    - Add e2e coverage for PodGroup creation and reference from Workload

/kind feature
/sig scheduling

title: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails
text: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails

title: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails
text:
### What happened?

While trying to use `kubectl diff` on Windows, I tried to specify the absolute path to the `diff.exe` shipped with Git Bash via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF`. This lead to the following error message:

```
error: failed to run "C:\\Program": executable file not found in %PATH%
```

title: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

`kubectl diff` should run `C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\diff.exe` when instructed to do so.

title: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Install Git on a Windows machine
2. Run the following PowerShell commands:
   ```powershell
   $env:KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF='C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\diff.exe'
   kubectl diff -f my_file.yaml
   ```

title: `kubectl diff` on Windows: Specifying a path with spaces to diff.exe via `KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF` fails
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

As an (ugly) workaround, one can use:

```powershell
$env:KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF='C:\PROGRA~1\Git\usr\bin\diff.exe'
```

(Assuming 8.3 file names are enabled.)

Any attempts to quote the path fail as well:
```powershell
$env:KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF='"C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\diff.exe"'
```
```powershell
$env:KUBECTL_EXTERNAL_DIFF="'C:\Program Files\Git\usr\bin\diff.exe'"
```

title: Add pv&pvc cacheManager to dswp in kubelet like secret&confimap
text:
Add pv&pvc cacheManager to dswp in kubelet like secret&confimap

For single node k8s cluster, there are 300+ pods running in the node, every pod has 10+ pvcs.   although all volumes haved been mounted and although many pods reference a same pvc,  but  every resync  per  about 1 minute, dswp should get 3000+ pvc and 3000+ pv from apiserver，on high io load scene, each get exhausts 500ms,  it would spent 3000s for all the syncs.  most are useless,  badly it can block new pod creating on pvc mount for long time.

so  can we add pv&pvc manager like secret&configmap? if so ,  all gets above from informers will enhance performance for kubelet

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Summary

A source-code audit of the kube-apiserver RBAC, admission, and authentication subsystems found that the default `edit` ClusterRole grants the `impersonate` verb on `serviceaccounts`, enabling namespace-scoped privilege escalation when privileged ServiceAccounts exist in the same namespace.

This behavior is acknowledged in code comments (`bootstrappolicy/policy.go:157` labels it an "escalating resource") and KEP 5284 introduces constrained impersonation as a replacement, but the risk is not prominently documented in the ClusterRole reference pages and most cluster operators are unaware.

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Impact

Any user with `edit` (via RoleBinding) in namespace X can:
1. Impersonate **any** ServiceAccount in namespace X via `Impersonate-User: system:serviceaccount:X:<name>`
2. Inherit all permissions of that ServiceAccount, including ClusterRoleBindings
3. If any SA in the namespace has `admin` or `cluster-admin` privileges (common for CI/CD, GitOps, and monitoring SAs), this is a direct privilege escalation path

The `admin` ClusterRole also inherits this rule via aggregation.

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text: # User has "edit" RoleBinding in namespace "production"

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
section: # "deploy-bot" SA in the same namespace has admin ClusterRoleBinding
text:
# "deploy-bot" SA in the same namespace has admin ClusterRoleBinding

kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:production:deploy-bot
kubectl get secrets -n production --as=system:serviceaccount:production:deploy-bot
kubectl create rolebinding escalate --clusterrole=admin \
  --user=attacker -n production \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:production:deploy-bot
```

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Scope Limitations

- Namespace-scoped only (RoleBinding in X does not grant impersonation in Y)
- SA-only (edit does not grant impersonate on users, groups, UIDs, or extras)
- Impersonation chaining is blocked (headers stripped before forwarding)

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Requests

1. **Documentation:** Add a prominent security note to the `edit` and `admin` ClusterRole reference documentation warning that these roles include ServiceAccount impersonation
2. **Hardening guidance:** Recommend operators audit SA bindings in namespaces where `edit`/`admin` is granted
3. **Consider removing `impersonate` from `editRules()`** in `bootstrappolicy/policy.go:159`, gated behind a feature flag if needed for backward compatibility
4. **Accelerate KEP 5284** constrained impersonation as the long-term replacement

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Code References

- Bootstrap policy rule: `plugin/pkg/auth/authorizer/rbac/bootstrappolicy/policy.go:159`
- Impersonation filter: `staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/impersonation.go:74-118`
- "Escalating resource" comment: `bootstrappolicy/policy.go:157`
- KEP 5284: constrained impersonation (`staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/endpoints/filters/impersonation/constrained_impersonation.go:41`)

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Full Audit Report

The complete audit (9 findings across RBAC, admission, and authn — 420 Go files reviewed) is published in the OSS Security Knowledge Base:

https://github.com/travis-burmaster/oss-security-kb/blob/main/wiki/kubernetes/kube-apiserver.md

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
## Additional Findings

The same audit identified three additional medium-severity issues in the admission control subsystem:
- Admission reinvocation skips newly-matching webhooks after label mutation
- Dry-run requests silently skip webhooks with `failurePolicy: Ignore` + `sideEffects: nil`
- Fail-open webhook bypass via induced timeout/network disruption

Details in the full audit report linked above.

/kind bug
/area security
/sig auth

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
If I can edit a ServiceAccount, I can obtain a token as it - is that right? I think it is.

It seems odd not to allow impersonating a ServiceAccount but retain the ability to elevate privileges through authenticating directly.

There are various answers to the problem but locking down access to the ServiceAccount itself is just as important as preventing impersonation.

title: edit ClusterRole grants namespace-scoped ServiceAccount impersonation — documentation + hardening request
text:
/close
The meaning of `edit` is for the subject to be able to run workloads in a namespace. Workloads are always allowed to have a ServiceAccount. Since the user can control the image of the pod, they have access to a token of a ServiceAccount, and so they don't need the impersonation at all.

Closing as a non-issue.

section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Current we keep the extension libraries added in Kubernetes located at: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/tree/master/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel/library
All the cost defined for the ext library is defined in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/cel/library/cost.go which is separately from the definition of func. Future contributors who is not familiar with the ext library would easily missing adding cost while adding new library. It would be great to have cost defined closer to the func.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Building on the previous discussions in issue #3949, I'd like to propose adding ability to live migrate a container from one node to another. I believe this would be a valuable addition to those of us that use Kubernetes as a compute platform on which to run large scale batch jobs and model training. I have read the KEP by @adrianreber , but it only covers the "first part" of the required functionality - aka checkpointing the container for further analysis - and is more security oriented. The proposal here would be more geared AI/ML workloads that are expensive to restart, prone to exhausting resources on a single node and would benefit from being able to be transferred to another node and restarted from the current state.

There are two specific cases I am thinking about:

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
1. Move container from one node to a node with more resources if container is exhausting resources on original node
2. Move container from one node to another is original node is a spot node and has been pre-empted

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text:
### Why is this needed?

There are two primary types of AI/ML workloads that run on Kubernetes - live services that run model inference and service requests from users and batch jobs/tasks that are launched by an orchestrator and usually make up a a part of a more involved ML pipeline. Examples of things that happen inside containers in these tasks are things like - extracting data from a data store, manipulating the data in a process inside the container, long-running ingestion process ( for example, embedding vectors and indexing them into a vector store) training a model either using some custom framework or an open source framework like scikit-learn or PyTorch, writing data back to a datastore or uploading a model file to object storage.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text:
Due to their nature, these tasks are expensive to restart. For example, terminating and restarting a model training container might mean that you lose hours of work. Unfortunately, due to the nature of these tasks, it can be difficult to estimate up front how much resources these tasks take and very frequently, these containers exhaust the resources available on a node. Due to the computational requirements of these tasks and the cost of cloud computing, spot instances would offer a way to architect these platform more cost-effectively were it not for the potential interruptions that these tasks would incur were a spot instance to be pre-empted.

Thus an easy-to use functionality that allows containers to be moved from one node to another would be very helpful in addressing some of the issues that make Kubernetes hard-to-use for AI/ML teams.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text:
To make this possible we are currently trying to set up a working group. https://github.com/kubernetes/community/pull/8508

We do not really see it as a technical challenge anymore. We are trying to figure out the correct way to add it to Kubernetes.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text: Thank you very much for the response @adrianreber. Is it possible to join the working group or at least participate as an observer in design discussions? I don't know much about Kubernetes internals, but I'm very curious about how the design of this functionality will look from the perspective of a k8s user.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text: Sure, the more the better. The WG has been approved and we are currently waiting for the necessary infrastructure to be created. Especially mailing list and slack channel. So that we have a place to discuss things. Just follow the ticket about the working group and you should see once the group has been officially created.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text:
Live migration will be hugely impactful, far more broadly than just for AI/ML.
* Allowing node refresh (deployment of host kernel security updates) without interrupting the very long-lived containers which are needed for interactive single-user sessions, such as for _Zero to JupyterHub_ (hosted coding environments). These containers may expect uptimes of a week or more (to prevent disrupting user's in-progress work, such as computation results that are cached in memory for interactive investigation but may be time-consuming to regenerate), conflicting with security policies which often mandate that latest kernel updates be deployed across fleets within 24-48 hours.
* Allowing efficient utilisation of temporarily excess host instances, by low-priority batch processes which may not implement their own checkpointing. It's not uncommon for AWS to provide compute instances at a 70-100% spot discount but then rescind them at very short notice after 10min-1hr. This is a mechanism for very cost-efficient use of avail

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text: able datacenter resources by non-urgent workloads, however it is difficult (unreasonable) for every different batch process to separately each implement frequent progress capture and automatic fault recovery, so retries may in practice need to repeatedly duplicate significant amounts (say 10m-1hr per interruption) of computation, which is an inefficient waste of resources (increasing greenhouse emissions etc).

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text:
This feature would be highly valuable and is a critical requirement for users operating not just AIML workloads, but also various types of Long-Running and Stateful Workloads in production, such as those utilizing Session Initiation Protocol (SIP).

Currently, many users managing Stateful Workloads during a Kubernetes upgrade often resort to a manual process: they monitor for dropped connections to trigger a graceful transition, allowing new sessions to naturally connect to the new Pods.

The ability to maintain a Pod's IP address during migration would significantly enhance the operational stability and user experience, completely eliminating the need for these complex, manual session-preservation workarounds. This is a crucial improvement for ensuring session persistence and reducing service disruption during cluster maintenance or scaling events.

title: Live container migration from one node to another to support long-running AI/ML workloads
text: We strongly support the development and implementation of this functionality. Furthermore, I am highly interested in contributing to the design process and would like to participate in the relevant SIG to help drive this feature forward.

title: kubelet marks the container Ready shortly which is killed for failing in StartupProbe
text:
### What happened?

When a pod has StartupProbe and LivenessProbe but without ReadinessProbe, the pod will be marked Ready shortly if the application exits without an error after receiving exit signal because of the failure in StartupProbe.

title: kubelet marks the container Ready shortly which is killed for failing in StartupProbe

text:

The container is always marked as NotReady.

title: kubelet marks the container Ready shortly which is killed for failing in StartupProbe
text:
It seems like the kubelet is marking containers as Ready even when there’s no readiness probe and the startup probe fails. Maybe the current logic sets Ready to true by default if readinessProbe is missing, which might not be correct in this case.
A rough idea could be to change that logic to keep containers NotReady if startup probe fails, regardless of the readiness probe presence. This might help the pod status reflect the actual container health better.

If this is true, than it will be a bug. But I doubt it is. Can it be that in your example startup probe succeeded?

title: Add support for built-in small object RWX Storage class
text: Add support for built-in small object RWX Storage class

title: Add support for built-in small object RWX Storage class
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Provide a builtin RWX supporting storage class. Potentially reusing the existing etcd store as a backend.

title: Add support for built-in small object RWX Storage class
text:
### Why is this needed?

Providing RWX based storage classes is a difficult endeavor, allowing write access from multiple sources at the same point. Historically various Linux filesystem tried to achieve this, with varying degree of success or complexity.

In the kubernetes world, pod-to-pod communication that is state based (pod A creates or configures something, pod B needs to have the resulting inforarmation) can be achieved, but needs reinvention in every app.

If kubernetes supported a builtin RWX storage class, even for small objects that are supported by etcd, it would allow a powerful new way of pods to submit information to each other.

Other ideas to solve the issue of pod-to-pod, stateful information would be to allow configmaps or secrets to be writable from pods.

title: Add support for built-in small object RWX Storage class
text: The suggested storageclass is mainly thought to be used to inject small pieces of information, such as configuration files, state of another pod ('I'm doing x already - use z to achieve something'), thus size restrictions of ~1MB per entity would not cause any issue.

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

When using [event record](https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/blob/v0.33.2/tools/record/event.go), the event object is created without EventTime populated, so this issue would like to evaluate the feasibility to make it optional so that the compatibility is guaranteed and no regression.

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text:
### Why is this needed?

`EventTime` provides greater time precision (in microseconds) than other timestamp fields (in seconds), which is very useful to do performance analysis based on events
