Curious, is there a good example in k/k of where we raise Event asynchronously so it is a problem? Or this is for external use cases?

So instead of "now" here: https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/blob/f2e6cadbe00d80a71ee6a7c145033e43526de0c3/tools/record/event.go#L485 you want to use an event time supplied by a caller? If this is the ask - I think we will need to be careful to check if there are any issues with the time from future or from very old past. I do not know if there are any other concerns, user supplied time sounds like a good idea.

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text:
Previous discussion which led to this issue's creation: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/132939#issuecomment-3074351056

I think there were concerns around modifying the existing core/v1 event recorder paths, and if `EventTime` is needed the recommendation was to migrate to using the events/v1 API instead

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text: @SergeyKanzhelev I would also think switching to events/v1 API instead, because `EventTime` only gives the accurate timestamp of **first** observed event, and we will need to update the schema for core/v1 event if we want the latest event timestamp.

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text: @richabanker @SergeyKanzhelev Wondering if migrating to events/v1 API will cause any backward compatibility issue?

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text:
> [@SergeyKanzhelev](https://github.com/SergeyKanzhelev) I would also think switching to events/v1 API instead, because `EventTime` only gives the accurate timestamp of **first** observed event, and we will need to update the schema for core/v1 event if we want the latest event timestamp.

kubelet does not provide any guarantee on specific events it exposes. Including their types. That's said it will be a stretch thinking that nobody took the dependency on it. That said I will be totally OK switching to the new API in kubelet, if we provide a feature gate option to opt-out for the old behavior for a version of two.

If API reviewers here have an opinion, please chime in.

Also if we will do this, let's do it early in 1.36 cycle so we have longer time to test it.

/sig node

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text:
I also found this old PR trying to migrate kubelet to the new API: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/100600

cc @dgrisonnet in case you also have any thoughts/historical context (perhaps [this](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/383#issuecomment-1234571434) previous comment is worth keeping in mind while attempting migration) since its also related to https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/3728

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text: @dgrisonnet were any progerss made since that comment in 2022?

title: [client-go] Optionally populate `EventTime` in event recorder for core/v1 events
text:
[This](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/383#issuecomment-1234571434) 30min refresh time is still [there](https://github.com/kubernetes/client-go/blob/45e0decafa9b847c983f55c84b4f6ce5617f8f69/tools/events/event_broadcaster.go#L51).

cc @SergeyKanzhelev so we basically want two sets of events logging mechanisms to work at the same time in the code but user can use flag to opt in a newer events type.

### What happened?

If external binding happen to a pod that is already in the scheduling queue and has either NNN or assumed NN (for instance waiting on permit) different from the binding one, other pods waiting on releasing these resources are not waken up by a deletion event.

title: kubepods.slice cgroups (memory and cpu) are reset every minute by kubelet
text:
kubepods.slice cgroups (memory and cpu) are reset every minute by kubelet

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137719/changes#diff-36d6d25926789aea821105963e22c1fe5fa85bc2a25531a8d295874a4c3a4aedR393-R395 removed the gating behind setting memory QoS, however later on https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137889 removed some of this logic due to it resetting the other pod cgroup properties. It seems like this is causing unintended side effects with the same behavior here. Since it was previously gated by an alpha feature gate, I think it makes sense to keep it that way.

title: kubepods.slice cgroups (memory and cpu) are reset every minute by kubelet
text:
/assign @liggitt 
/assign @SergeyKanzhelev 
/cc @sohankunkerkar

title: kubepods.slice cgroups (memory and cpu) are reset every minute by kubelet
text:
/priority critical-urgent
/triage accepted

I am setting critical urgent as it is breaking previously working environments.

Specifically, we see that some values like `cpu.max` under `/sys/fs/cgroup/kubepods.slice/` may got reset back to max even if previously set to specific value by outside process. Even though managing kubelet-owned cgroups is not a good idea, there are  environments where this is done. And we need to rollout this change (if required) with caution.

title: kubepods.slice cgroups (memory and cpu) are reset every minute by kubelet
text:
> Specifically, we see that some values like `cpu.max` under `/sys/fs/cgroup/kubepods.slice/` got reset back to max

I agree that side-effect of unconditionally reconciling _memory_ cgroup values is unexpected, and that moving back into a gate-protected off-by-default block is a good idea.

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text:
## Describe the bug

My team provisions rabbitmq clusters on several openshift platforms.
We have several kuberentes stages: test,npr,prd.

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text:
About a month ago **we updated the rabbitmq container images** running on the test platform (bumped image from 4.1.3 to 4.2.4). That platform has only 2 rabbitmq clusters ("rmq-test-a" and "rmq-test-b", each deployed in its own namesapce with 1 replica/1PVC).
Everything ran fine for rmq-test-a, but something very strange happened for rmq-test-b.
For rmq-test-a, the pods were restarted and got the new image version, as you would expect.
But for rmq-test-b, **we saw an extra (second) pod being created** (despite replica=1) and a new PVC being created for that pod as well. According to the logs, that second pod (which should not exist) was started with the new image 4.2.4 (while the other one still was running on 4.1.3). After a while the first pod got the new image too and the second pod got deleted (however the PVC is still lying there).
We can clearly see from the logs, the metrics etcc.. that an extra pod appeared, **thus violating the replica=1 spec**.

We did not notice that issue at the time.

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text:
We did not notice that issue while updating the image on the npr openshift platforms.

However we did notice it again while updating the image on one of the prd platform. This time for a rabbitmqcluster with 3 replica, which then got a fourth one for about 5 minutes (with a fourth pvc). The fourth pod is gone, but the 4th PVC is sill there.

How do you explain (or reproduce) this ??

**Quick word about our setup**:
Our RabbitmqClusters are deployed via argocd. Each customer rabbimq instance (so basically the rabbitmqcluster yaml file) is stored in github and argocd picks it up and applies it to the correct platofmr/namespace

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text:
## To Reproduce

Haven't been able to reproduce it yet

**Expected behavior**
Upon an update of the spec, the number of replica should be respected at all time (well obviously you can have less because of rolling update etc..) but absolutely not more replica than requested which can lead to tons of different issues, especially now that we have a fourth PVC lying there.

title: statefulset repliocas mismatch for a rabbitmqcluster
text: ## Screenshots Example of an update that ran well: you can see clearly that there is a version change around the 19.03 (that was one of the good guys on the test platform)

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
As a result of #124216, which was introduced in v.1.32, a pod CPU limit calculated in `ResourceConfigForPod()` is rounded up to the nearest 10ms in `libcontainer` at resizing the pod:
- Resize a pod:
  ```
  $ kubectl patch pod resize-pod --subresource=resize --patch '{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"resize-container", "resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"417m"}}}]}}'
  pod/resize-pod patched
  ```
- The container cgroup value is set with 1ms precision:
  ```
  $ kubectl exec resize-pod -- cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.max
  41700 100000
  ```
- The pod cgroup value is rounded up:
  ```
  $ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod68a17b59_0d31_40b2_ba86_ea43f3b2f05c.slice/cpu.max
  42000 100000
  ```

When `systemd` cgroup driver is used, `libcontainer` passes the CPU Quota to `systemd` with rounding up:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a4b8a3b2e33a3b591884f69b64f439e6b880dc40/vendor/github.com/opencontainers/runc/l

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
ibcontainer/cgroups/systemd/common.go#L304-L311

In addition, there seems to be a race in `libcontainer`. It directly writes values to the cgroup file without roundup after it passes the rounded value to `systemd`:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a4b8a3b2e33a3b591884f69b64f439e6b880dc40/vendor/github.com/opencontainers/runc/libcontainer/cgroups/systemd/v2.go#L489-L493

So, there is also a case where the cgroup value is set as calculated. As far as I tried, decreasing CPU limits usually hits this case though I’m not sure why:
- Decrease the CPU limits:
  ```
  $ kubectl patch pod resize-pod --subresource=resize --patch '{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"resize-container", "resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"365m"}}}]}}'
  pod/resize-pod patched
  ```

- Both the container and the pod cgroup values are set with 1ms precision:
  ```
  $ kubectl exec resize-pod -- cat /sys/fs/cgroup/cpu.max
  36500 100000
  $ cat /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod68a17b59_0d31_40b2_ba86_ea43f3b2f05c.slice/cpu.max
  36500 100000
  ```

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

This roundup looks like the intended behavior of `systemd` cgroup driver because CPU quota is also rounded up when a pod is just created with 1ms precision CPU limits. However, I have the following concerns:
- We might need to confirm this tiny gap doesn’t cause a similar issue to #128769 at resizing pods.
- We might need to clarify why the CPU quota of pod cgroup is sometimes not rounded up. This is especially necessary to complete #127192, which is going to add pod cgroup verification to resize tests.

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

0. Use `systemd` cgroup driver and enable `InPlacePodVertialScaling`.
1. Resize CPU limits of a pod with 1ms precision.

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
[FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
I watched the `cpu.max` with `tail -f`.

When the CPU limit was increased from `200m` to `417m`, the rounded value was eventually set:
```
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
20000 100000
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
42000 100000
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
41700 100000
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
42000 100000
```

When the CPU limit is decreased from `417m` to `365m`, the rou

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
nded value was not set:
```
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
42000 100000
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
37000 100000
tail: /sys/fs/cgroup/kubelet.slice/kubelet-kubepods.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable.slice/kubelet-kubepods-burstable-pod9f6e542c_cbd9_4740_8f8b_4eac466f26d2.slice/cpu.max: file truncated
36500 100000
```

It seems that:
1. The rounded value is once set along with `setUnitProperties()`:
	https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a4b8a3b2e33a3b591884f69b64f439e6b880dc40/vendor/github.com/opencontainers/runc/libcontainer/cgroups/systemd/v2.go#L489
2. The original value is set by `fsMgr.Set()`:
	https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/a4b8a3b2e33a3b591884f69b64f439e6

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
b880dc40/vendor/github.com/opencontainers/runc/libcontainer/cgroups/systemd/v2.go#L493
3. Only at increasing the limit, the rounded value is set again. I guess `cpu.max` is updated by `systemd` along with updating `cpu.weight` because `cpu.weight` (CPUShares) is also increased after the CPU limit is increased even if the CPU request is not updated:
   https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8f330c63688ab920ea8d864d9a3193da535018e5/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L723-L727

The unnecessary increase for the pod CPU request is caused by round down at calculating CPU shares from CPU weight. At this comparison, the current value is calculated from the actual `cpu.weight` while the new value is calculated from the pod spec:
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8f330c63688ab920ea8d864d9a3193da535018e5/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L723

In case where the pod CPU request is `100ms`, the new CPU shares is `102`. This CPU shares is converted to `4` as CPU weight. `4

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
` is converted to `80` as the current CPU shares by `cpuWeightToCPUShares()`: 
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8f330c63688ab920ea8d864d9a3193da535018e5/pkg/kubelet/cm/cgroup_v2_manager_linux.go#L175-L176

This difference (`102` vs `80`) causes the unnecessary trial to increase the CPU weight at the pod cgroup though the CPU weight is eventually overwritten with the current value.

title: [FG:InPlacePodVerticalScaling] Pod CPU limit is not configured to cgroups as calculated if systemd cgroup driver is used
text:
I think the two fixes are necessary:
- `libcontainer` should write the rounded value to `cpu.max`. It might be better to stop updating the file directly if it is really unnecessary:
  https://github.com/opencontainers/runc/blob/d48d9cfefcdc13862ade35fd2df428cf9798bf22/libcontainer/cgroups/systemd/v2.go#L492
- At resizing a pod cgroup, CPU requests should be compared after being converted to CPU weight values in order to avoid unnecessary requests to `systemd`:
  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/8f330c63688ab920ea8d864d9a3193da535018e5/pkg/kubelet/kuberuntime/kuberuntime_manager.go#L797

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text:
### What happened?

During the scheduling cycle if pod gets recreated with the same name during filtering there is a potential race condition causing the pod to stay in inFlightPods/inFlightEvents forever. 

Exact steps:

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text:
## Step 1: The Initial Lock-in

- Pop: The scheduler pops Pod-X (UID: AAAA) from the top of the scheduling queue.
- Record: The queue pushes Pod-X to the tail of the mixed doubly-linked `inFlightEvents` list to track when it started.
- Map: The queue stores the exact memory pointer to that list node inside `inFlightPods[AAAA]`.

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text:
## Step 2: The Synchronous Failure

- Execute: The scheduler begins running synchronous Filter plugins (e.g. checking NodeSelector, Taints, or custom filters).
- Fail: One of the filter plugins returns Unschedulable.
- Abort: Because scheduling cannot proceed, the scheduler aborts the cycle immediately and jumps straight to the failure handler. It skips `sched.SchedulingQueue.Done(assumedPod.UID)` done before bindingCycle.

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
section: ## Step 3: The Recreation Overwrite (The Race Condition)
text:
## Step 3: The Recreation Overwrite (The Race Condition)

- Delete & Recreate: Just milliseconds before the failure handler runs, an external controller deletes Pod-X and immediately recreates a brand new instance with the exact same name/namespace, generating a new UID (BBBB).
- Fetch: The failure handler queries the local Informer cache for the latest state of the pod using its string name (Pod-X).
- Retrieve: The cache naturally returns the newly created instance with UID BBBB.
- Overwrite: The internal reference in the scheduler is updated to hold the new pod reference (BBBB).

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text:
## Step 4: Missing the Target

- Queue Push: The failure handler attempts to put the pod back into the queue using `AddUnschedulableIfNotPresent(BBBB)`.
- Deferred Done: The very first line of `AddUnschedulableIfNotPresent` function unconditionally executes:
``` go
       // In any case, this Pod will be moved back to the queue and we should call Done.
	defer p.Done(pInfo.Pod.UID)
```
- The Miss: `Done(BBBB)` looks inside the `inFlightPods` tracking map, sees that UID BBBB does not exist, and does absolutely nothing.
- The Ghost: The original UID (AAAA) is entirely forgotten by the system and remains permanently trapped inside the `inFlightPods` map and the doubly-linked list!

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
text:
# Step 5: The Catastrophic Dam

- Drift: Over the next few seconds, as other unrelated pods complete their cycles normally, they get removed from the front of the list. The trapped ghost pod (AAAA) eventually shifts all the way to the Front() position of the list.
- The Blockade: Because `Done(AAAA)` is never called, this node can never be removed from Front().
- Paralysis: Every time the cleanup routine attempts to run, it looks at the very first element of the list, sees the ghost pod (AAAA), and completely halts all pruning to protect it.
- The Explosion: Meanwhile, standard background updates (Node usage, endpoint slices, system leases) continuously append thousands of new *clusterEvent nodes to the tail of the linked list. Because the front node is anchored forever, none of those incoming events can ever be deleted—causing your InFlightEvents metric to grow infinitely

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

The Done method of scheduling queue should be called on the old UID.

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
section: ### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?
text:
### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Integration test `TestInFlightPodLeak` added in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/compare/master...Argh4k:kubernetes:potential-memory-leak reproduces that locally.

title: Race in scheduler can cause infinite growth of inFlightEvents
section: ### Anything else we need to know?
text:
### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

text:
### Kubernetes version

Observed on a cluster running v1.34.1, but still reproducible with the HEAD.

text:
### Cloud provider

title: Kubelet regression in 1.31 where pods get stuck in RunContainerError on OS with cgroupv1
text: Kubelet regression in 1.31 where pods get stuck in RunContainerError on OS with cgroupv1

title: Kubelet regression in 1.31 where pods get stuck in RunContainerError on OS with cgroupv1
text:
### What happened?

We observed a Kubelet regression in `1.31.1400` where pods get stuck in `RunContainerError` on Rocky Linux 8.6 OS which uses cgroup v1. While upgrading Kubernetes from 1.30 to 1.31, we observe the issue once we put the node out of maintenance mode.

The issue is observed with Containerd 1.7 and 1.6 as well.

While K8s 1.31 is out of support, is there a code pointer or commit that may have changed the behavior in cgroup?

The following kernel settings currently resolve the issue:
  * Set the `cgroup.memory=nokmem` config during the OS upgrade.
  * Reboot the node as part of the OS upgrade process.
  * Upgrade K8s to 1.31

Is there any advice against it?

title: Kubelet regression in 1.31 where pods get stuck in RunContainerError on OS with cgroupv1
section: ### What did you expect to happen?
text:
### What did you expect to happen?

in 1.31, cgroupv1 is not yet deprecated https://kubernetes.io/blog/2024/08/13/kubernetes-v1-31-release/#cgroup-v1-enters-the-maintenance-mode  (although it was put in maintenance mode), Kubelet should not yet be unhappy with cgroupv1 until it is fully deprecated. The expectation is that this error should not happen in Kubelet 1.31

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
section: ### What would you like to be added?
text:
### What would you like to be added?

Current default eviction threshold is hardcoded as 100MiB, this works for default linux default (66MiB). But this can be adjusted, and with swap use-cases, kubernetes environments will likely see this sysctl parameter adjusted to a higher value than default.

This proposal suggests to set the sysctl parameter based on the observed value instead:

`max( DefaultHardEvictionThresholdValue , NodeMinFreeKbytes + SafetyMargin )`

Where:
- `DefaultHardEvictionThresholdValue` is the current default (100MiB).
- `NodeMinFreeKbytes` is the value read from /proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes, converted to MiB.
- `SafetyMargin` is a buffer (e.g., 50MiB or a percentage of min_free_kbytes) to ensure eviction triggers comfortably before the kernel's absolute minimum is reached. 

Proposal discussed at SIG-Node: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1qBkCuB7hPbCfCkTc7ilYwwDzyG_ykpP7losidyuOiTU/edit?usp=sharing

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
### Why is this needed?

This is required to ensure Kubernetes handles eviction before the OOM killer starts handling for memory pressure. This is particularly important for swap use-cases as the swap watermarks are directly influenced by this sysctl parameter.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
Hey @ajaysundark! Thanks for raising this!

I definitely think this is important.
The `100Mi` is just a guess, and although not a particularly bad one, it is not optimized per the system it's running on and might lead to a situation where evictions are de-facto disabled.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
Some general thoughts / questions:
1. AFAIK `/proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes` represents the minimum amount of memory to keep free. However, the kernel will later distribute this amount between different zones, e.g. DMA, etc. Do we want to instead use the watermarks defined for the "Normal" memory zone?
2. You wrote: `"with swap use-cases, kubernetes environments will likely see this sysctl parameter adjusted to a higher value than default"`. I'm interested in this use-case. It is true that modifying `min_free_kbytes` will affect the thresholds for swap, however are you aware of scenarios in which people need to increase the value for swap there? in which scenarios are admins likely to do so?
3. If a user sets the eviction threshold to be below the watermark/min_free_kbytes value he would affectively disable evictions. Should we error out or at least warn in this scenario?

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
BTW, I have explained watermarks & swap for an outdated version of the swap KEP (which was ended up not being merged). Perhaps you'll find it valuable: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/b2719f0ad6fee5a0dc441dcdffd0f55df0b20c76/keps/sig-node/2400-node-swap/README.md#advanced-best-practices-for-manually-setting-memory-evictions.

Thank you!

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
> BTW, I have explained watermarks & swap for an outdated version of the swap KEP

Thanks for your response and sharing this earlier proposal, @iholder101!

Your explanation [here](https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/b2719f0ad6fee5a0dc441dcdffd0f55df0b20c76/keps/sig-node/2400-node-swap/README.md#advanced-best-practices-for-manually-setting-memory-evictions) captures the general idea correctly that the memory reclaim are triggered by different watermarks. However, there are some crucial details I could add to capture the influence by `min_free_kbytes`, and why this proposal is needed:

The standard watermarks are `min`, `low`, and `high`. Their relationship is: `pages_high > pages_low > pages_min` --  your proposal incorrectly captured this as `min` over `low`. 
The trigger for `kswapd` (indirect reclaim) is the "low" watermark, and "min" threshold is for direct reclaim (IIUC, this is not handled by `kswapd`).

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
**How `min_free_kbytes` come into picture:**
[Kernel calculates](https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/9d7a0577c9db35c4cc52db90bc415ea248446472/mm/page_alloc.c#L6116) the `pages_min`, `pages_low`, and `pages_high` values based on zone size and this crucial tunable parameter `/proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes` which sets the baseline for the watermarks.

```
// overly simplified to show the parameter impact..
`pages_min` ~= min_free_kbytes / 'kbytes_per_page'
`pages_low` ~= pages_min + (pages_min / 4 ... or scaled-on-size).
`pages_high` ~= pages_min + (pages_min / 2 ... or scaled-on-size).
```

`min_free_kbytes` thus largely influence the base-line for reclaim.

You also had similar thoughts on your earlier proposal -
>  For example, the threshold can be set to a value between the low and min watermarks.

My opinion is that kubelet should try to protect this as default behavior protecting node from stability risks.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
>  Bear in mind that the default memory eviction threshold of 100Mi is between the low and min watermarks as well, which means that by default evictions are expected to work decently.

I think this assumption may be incorrect. k8s should not rely upon few observations and set a static-value.  For eg: [one reference I found](https://docs.azul.com/prime/zst/vm.min-free-settings) was suggesting best-practice as `min_free_kbytes=1GB`.
 
> 
> 1. AFAIK `/proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes` represents the minimum amount of memory to keep free. However, the kernel will later distribute this amount between different zones, e.g. DMA, etc. Do we want to instead use the watermarks defined for the "Normal" memory zone?

You're technically correct that `min_free_kbytes` is distributed across zones. "Normal" is where most user pod memory resides.  Since the zone watermarks are derived from min_free_kbytes, I'd think using min_free_kbytes as a proxy is a reasonable and practical approach.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text: > 2. You wrote: `"with swap use-cases, kubernetes environments will likely see this sysctl parameter adjusted to a higher value than default"`. I'm interested in this use-case. It is true that modifying `min_free_kbytes` will affect the thresholds for swap, however are you aware of scenarios in which people need to increase the value for swap there? in which scenarios are admins likely to do so?

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
The primary motivation increasing `min_free_kbytes` is avoiding node/kernel crashes by keeping a larger buffer and reducing OOM killer risks by also raising the kube eviction-threshold defaults. 
This also enables proactive swap utilization,  as raising min_free_kbytes also proportionally raises pages_low and pages_high as well. When swap rate couldn't match the memory consumption rate this will likely end up in frequent direct reclaim and performance collapse, in which case 'early swapping' may be preferred.  This is especially crucial in a HDD where the swap I/O latency could be high.
  
> 3. If a user sets the eviction threshold to be below the watermark/min_free_kbytes value he would affectively disable evictions. Should we error out or at least warn in this scenario?>

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text: I think k8s approach should be to protect the node by default, triggering Kubelet eviction before the kernel likely enters direct reclaim. However, if a user explicitly configures a lower `evictionHard[memory.available]` threshold (i.e., one that is less than NodeMinFreeKbytes + SafetyMargin), this explicit configuration should be respected. They may have fine-tuned node stability through other means or rely on different mechanisms for memory management.  Kubelet should emit a clear warning event if the configured eviction threshold is lower than the calculated default eviction-threshold. IMO, Erroring out would likely be too restrictive.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
Some notes from the SIG Node meeting:

1. There are more configuration settings that are not properly configured for kubelet. Like reserved (system and kube) memory, cpu, etc. Which will effectively effect this behavior in similar way. I bet there are more settings that do not have "proper" defaults that work universally. So from the precedence perspective, "good" defaults was never something that kubelet was concerned about.
2. What about ultra small nodes? What other limitations this value might have?
3. What the behavior will be when kubelet fails to obtain the value? What if the obtained value is simply wrong (max_int) or `0` due to wrong reading? Will kubelet start be delayed for the duration of metrics calculations? All those reliability concerns needs to be addressed.
4. What about Windows?

In general, changing this value can always be done "externally", there is not a huge priority for implementing this even if there is a great formulae we can find in discussions here.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
@SergeyKanzhelev Thank you for raising these points.

A few thoughts on this:
1. The current default value of `100Mi` works pretty well on many systems, but on very large or very small systems it would become problematic.
	* If the system is large enough: the kernel would start OOM killing before the `100Mi` threshold is met, therefore evictions are effectively disabled.
	* If the system is small enough: The default `100Mi` can lead to memory being wasted, or worse, for the node to become unschedulable.
2. This would be especially handy for swap use-cases, as `min_free_kbytes` has a great effect to the threshold in which pages start to be swapped to disk.
3. We can declare this as a best-effort, meaning if there's a timeout or an erroneous value, we'll fallback to `100Mi`.
4. Perhaps there's some kind of a Windows equivalent to this, but I'm really not sure.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
Thanks @SergeyKanzhelev and @iholder101 for the feedback! 
  
> 1. There are more configuration settings that are not properly configured for kubelet. Like reserved (system and kube) memory, cpu, etc. Which will effectively effect this behavior in similar way. I bet there are more settings that do not have "proper" defaults that work universally. So from the precedence perspective, "good" defaults was never something that kubelet was concerned about.

While other Kubelet defaults exist, the eviction threshold is fundamental to node stability, warranting an adaptive default.

This is particularly important for eviction behavior in swap scenarios as this value is used to calculate WMARK_LOW, which is the trigger for swap.  Linux kernel adaptively scales 'min_free_kbytes' based on system RAM, aligning the default eviction threshold with this kernel value would be a more appropriate base-line.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text: My thoughts on this are, if k8s user adjusts this for desired swapping behavior, the default eviction trigger should be adjusted accordingly. > 2. What about ultra small nodes? Linux kernel selects `min_free_kbytes` defaults based on size, proportional to the 'free memory available'. If k8s is considering eviction should protect the OOM behavior appropriately in small nodes, our defaults *should* be based on the observed value not a static-value. below plot based on 'kernel calculated defaults' (values from https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/bc3372351d0c8b2726b7d4229b878342e3e6b0e8/mm/page_alloc.c#L6204): > 3. Will kubelet start be delayed / what if this value cannot be read.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
IMO, this shouldn't add startup-delay for kubelet, but failures should revert to current-default. For eg: kubelet has permission errors accessing file `/proc/sys/vm/min_free_kbytes`, it would set 'NodeMinFreeKbytes' as 0, and `'max( DefaultHardEvictionThresholdValue , NodeMinFreeKbytes + SafetyMargin )'`, would set this to current defaults (100MiB). Kubelet has precedence for reading sysctl parameters already on startup: eg: [1](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9ba7dcecc39274f3acf4064d572996ecdbc96f10/pkg/util/kernel/version.go#L36), [2](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9ba7dcecc39274f3acf4064d572996ecdbc96f10/pkg/kubelet/stats/pidlimit/pidlimit_linux.go#L41), [3](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/9ba7dcecc39274f3acf4064d572996ecdbc96f10/pkg/kubelet/cm/container_manager_linux.go#L413-L426)

> 4. Windows

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
I'm not familiar with Windows OOM behavior, but this should be non-disruptive for Windows as (3) would also apply here, the file will be non-existent in Windows and will revert this to current defaults. Also to add swap use-cases dont apply to Windows.

> We can declare this as a best-effort

+1, this proposal doesn't intend to replace the min defaults, but should be adaptive also considering when user set high value for this parameter.

> If the system is small enough: The default 100Mi can lead to memory being wasted,

agree to this sentiment, but this is the current-behavior in k8s. I expect for existing small footprint systems, this could already been handled explicitly by setting eviction-Threshold to an appropriate value.

> How important is it to automatically do it vs give advice and let users configure?

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text: I guess my inclination would be that kubelet could handle this for better swap interests. But if this is seen as for advanced users whocan handle it themselves and should go into documentation (if there will be a how-to swap tuning?), I think I'm okay with that.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
If free memory is near vm.min_free_kbytes, kubelet itself is likely stuck at direct memory reclamation.
It maybe too late for kubelet to evict a pod.

I think it is important to ensure `systemReserved` of kubelet is larger than `vm.min_free_kbytes`.

title: Default memory eviction-hard threshold in kubernetes should be set more than `vm.min_free_kbytes`
text:
Enterprise-grade systems now frequently have hundreds of GB of RAM and multiple 25, 40, or even 100 GB NICs.

In our prod environment `vm.min_free_kbytes` is 2% of `MemTotal` of /proc/meminfo, mainly for two reasons:
1. avoid memory fragmentation
2. try to reserve enough memory for a network burst(in memcg-v1, TCP memory is accounted separately from the memcg memory usage). See [Reserving sufficient kernel memory](https://github.com/amzn/amzn-drivers/blob/master/kernel/linux/ena/ENA_Linux_Best_Practices.rst#reserving-sufficient-kernel-memory).

'2%' maybe a lot of memory. 40 GB for a node with 2 TB memory.
